title: BUG: linalg: wrong handling of args/kwargs in `_apply_over_batch`
body:
### Describe your issue.

As far as I can tell, version 1.16 introduced the [_apply_over_batch](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/6ed0250035135c0fd6717ae4caf2f8d270c3eb90/scipy/_lib/_util.py#L1101) wrapper around many of the [scipy.linalg solvers](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/main/scipy/linalg/_solvers.py), e.g., [solve_continuous_are](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/6ed0250035135c0fd6717ae4caf2f8d270c3eb90/scipy/linalg/_solvers.py#L343).

I discovered the issue when calling `solve_continuous_are`. When called with keyword arguments

```python
solve_continuous_are(A, B, Q, R, s=S)
```

the arguments seem to be passed by the wrapper `_apply_over_batch` to the original method `solve_continuous_are` in the wrong order. In the case above, argument `s` is passed as argument `e`. This leads to the exception `"Matrix e should be square."` in [_are_validate_args](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/6ed0250035135c0fd6717ae4caf2f8d270c3eb90/scipy/linalg/_solvers.py#L758) because `S` was passed as `e` (instead of `s`) to `solve_continuous_are`. The problem does not occur when calling explicitly with all arguments, e.g.,

```python
solve_continuous_are(A, B, Q, R, e=None, s=S)
```

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
from scipy.linalg import solve_continuous_are

P_exp = np.array(
    [
        [0.502193051922548, -0.016892161794694],
        [-0.016892161794694, 0.330569037292075],
    ]
)
A = np.array([[-0.9, 0.25], [0, -1.1]])
B = np.array([[0.23], [0.45]])
Q = np.eye(2)
R = np.atleast_2d(0.45)
S = np.array([[0.1], [0.2]])

P = solve_continuous_are(A, B, Q, R, s=S)

np.testing.assert_allclose(P, P_exp)
```

### Error message

```shell
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test.py", line 16, in <module>
    P = solve_continuous_are(A, B, Q, R, s=S)
        ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "python3.11/site-packages/scipy/_lib/_util.py", line 1226, in wrapper
    return f(*arrays, *other_args, **kwargs)
           ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "python3.11/site-packages/scipy/linalg/_solvers.py", line 463, in solve_continuous_are
    a, b, q, r, e, s, m, n, r_or_c, gen_are = _are_validate_args(
                                              ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "python3.11/site-packages/scipy/linalg/_solvers.py", line 845, in _are_validate_args
    raise ValueError("Matrix e should be square.")
ValueError: Matrix e should be square.
```

title: DOC: optimize.minimize: erroneously claims COBYLA doesn't support equality constraints
body:
### Issue with current documentation:

The documentation for scipy.optimize.minimize contains the following text in the descriptoin of constraints:

```
Note that COBYLA only supports inequality constraints.
```

As of 1.16.0, this is no longer the case.  The following example runs successfully and issues no exception as earlier versions would have:

```python
from scipy.optimize import minimize

# Define the objective function to minimize
def objective(x):
    """Minimize f(x,y) = x^2 + y^2"""
    return x[0]**2 + x[1]**2

# Define constraint functions
def constraint1(x):
    """Constraint: x + y == 1"""
    return x[0] + x[1] - 1

# Initial guess
x0 = [1.0, 0.0]

# Set up constraints
constraints = [
    {'type': 'eq', 'fun': constraint1}
]

# Run optimization
result = minimize(
    objective,
    x0,
    method='COBYLA',
    constraints=constraints,
    options={
        'maxiter': 1000,  # Maximum iterations
        'rhobeg': 1.0,    # Initial step size
        'tol': 1e-6,      # Tolerance for convergence
        'disp': True      # Display convergence messages
    }
)

# Display results
print("Optimization Results:")
print(f"Success: {result.success}")
print(f"Status: {result.status}, {result.message}")
print(f"Optimal solution: x = {result.x[0]:.12f}, y = {result.x[1]:.12f}")

```

title: DOC: Incorrect dimension of PPoly.c in docstring
body:
### Issue with current documentation:

For a PPoly of degree `k`, the shape of the coefficients array is  (k+1, m, ...) and not `(k, m, ...)` as currently written.
( https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.interpolate.PPoly.html ).

In accordance with standard definition of polynomial degree, a polynomial of degree k has k+1 coefficients.
This can also be verified by comparison with the formula that appears just above the parameter definitions, in which the  loop index `m` goes over `k+1` (not k) different values.

title: ENH: linalg: Follow-up tasks for ILP64 support
body:
gh-22743 laid the groundwork for 64-bit integer (ILP64) support. Many SciPy submodules can now switch to supporting ILP64, here's a tracker:

- `scipy.linalg`: `f2py`-based functions use `get_{blas,lapack}_funcs`, which default to `ilp64=False`. Either update individual calls to `get_lapack_funs`, or switch the default to `ilp64="preferred"`, https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.linalg.get_lapack_funcs.html 
   
### Extending source-level support

C extensions need to change the `dependencies: [lapack_lp64_dep]` to `dependencies: [lapack_dep]` and make their C code 64-bit int compatible:

- [x] `scipy.linalg._internal_matfuncs` -- done in https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/24829
.
- [x] `scipy.integrate._odepack` -- done in https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/24873
- [x] `scipy.integrate._vode` -- done in https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/24873
.
- [x] `scipy.optimize._slsqplib` -- done in https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/24703
- [x] `scipy.optimize._lbfgs` -- done in https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/24703
- [x] `scipy.optimize._trlib` -- https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/24756
.
- [x] `scipy.sparse.linalg._superlu` -- done in https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/24875
- [x] `scipy.sparse.linalg._arpacklib` -- done in https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/24844
- [x] `scipy.sparse.linalg._propack` -- done in https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/24841
.
- [x] `scipy.special._ellip_harm_2` -- done in https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/24871
- [x] `scipy.special.cython_special`  -- same as `_ellip_harm_2`, also done in [gh-24871](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/24871)
- [x] `scipy.special._ufuncs`  -- same as `_ellip_harm_2`, also done in [gh-24871](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/24871)

Fortran extensions additionally need to use integer size flags (or get converted to C):

- [ ] `scipy.odr.__odrpack` : deprecated, can ignore (EDIT: to be removed after 1.18.x branch creation)


### Extending BLAS library support and CI coverage

- [x] Add Accelerate ILP64 support: done in gh-23399
- [ ] Add FlexiBLAS CI job and ILP64 support (see NumPy's `linux_blas.yml`)

### Related topics/requirements

- [x] Build system support, tracked separately in gh-17244.
    - [x] Taken care of in gh-24977 and precursor PRs. Docs at https://scipy.github.io/devdocs/building/blas_lapack.html#bit-integer-ilp64-blas-lapack
- [x] Dealing with the need to _also_ link LP64 due to re-exporting it in `cython_blas`/`cython_lapack`: tracked separately in gh-21889

title: DOC: spatial.is_valid_dm: misleading documentation
body:
### Issue with current documentation:

Current implemenation checks symmetry and zero diagonal which are necessary conditions for a distance matrix, but not sufficient, as the **triangle inequality** is not checked

Current naming (`is_valid_dm`) and documentation may suggest the function checks for all distance matrix properties, including the triangle inequality. This clarification will help users avoid misinterpreting the function's behavior.

_No response_

title: Chatterjee's Xi returns different results based on `numpy` major version
body:
### Describe your issue.

# Issue
Depending on the input data, the coefficient and p-value results of running `scipy.stats.chatterjeexi()` often returns different results based on whether the version of `numpy` installed is >=2.0.0 or <2.0.  Those using `numpy>=2.0` seem to be more correct, but this has not been verified. This is unusual because other correlation functions' coefficients, like Pearson's R and Spearman's Rho, don't have changing coefficients based on numpy's version. And, according to [Chatterjee (2021)](https://doi.org/10.1080/01621459.2020.1758115), this coefficient should not either. 

The _Xi_ coefficient should be between 0-1, but using a proprietary dataset (that I cannot share here) of 14,366 observations, the results using the identical data from the same dataset in the same order are as follows:

> Scipy v1.16.0 with Numpy at v1.26.4 ran Chatterjee's Xi and got a corr=1.2256     w/ P-value=0.0

versus

> Scipy with Numpy at v2.0.0 ran Chatterjee's Xi and got a corr=0.2087*** w/ P-value=0.0

The only variable changed here is the numpy version. The latter with v2.0.0 seems more appropriate, both because it is within the range Chatterjee sets for appropriate calculation of his coefficient, and it lines up a bit better with Spearman's Rho coefficient for the same two variables. 


# Cause
My best guess, based on my limited testing, is that this arises from the following code in scipy.stats._correlation.py:

https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/9ae478ddaec6e1a71ca31624199e1ea171835eec/scipy/stats/_correlation.py#L14-L24

The `numpy==2.0.0` [release notes](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/releases/tag/v2.0.0#:~:text=Minor%20changes%20in%20behavior%20of%20sorting%20functions) detail that 

> methods that aren't stable may return slightly different results in 2.0.0 compared to 1.26.x. This includes the default method of numpy.argsort. 

Testing shows that the Xi statistic's computing using numpy's argsort, broadcast_arrays, and take_along_axis all differ based on numpy's version as 1.26.x vs 2.0.0. Because _Xi_ is a rank-order coefficient, my hunch is that the ordering of one or all of these functions differs because of the introduction of `stable` in numpy v2.0 to the `argsort` function. I'm not sophisticated enough to thoroughly test this, though. 

# Notes

- Chatterjee's Xi was introduced in `scipy v1.15.0`, https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/21453. 
- I included a test dataset below that will reproduce the error, albeit with oddly negative coefficients. I'm aware that mathematically, this can happen, as noted in both the paper itself and a competing python implementation of calculating Xi: https://github.com/swarnakumar/xicorpy/issues/32#issuecomment-2253698637. Maybe not the best example dataset(?), but it's still odd and changes the coefficient based on the numpy version.
- Forgive me if issues pertaining to `numpy` versioning are handled differently in this package.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from scipy import stats as sps, __version__ as spv

non_iid_df = pd.DataFrame(
    {
        "X": [1, 1, 3, 3, 5, 5, 7, 7, 9, 9, 11, 11, 13, 13, 15, 15, 17, 17, 19, 19, 21, 21, 23, 23, 25, 25, 27, 27, 29, 29, 31, 31, 33, 33, 35, 35, 37, 37, 39, 39, 41, 41, 43, 43, 45, 45, 47, 47, 49, 49, 51, 51, 53, 53, 55, 55, 57, 57, 59, 59, 61, 61, 63, 63, 65, 65, 67, 67, 69, 69],
        "Y": [5, 6, 5, 6, 5, 6, 5, 6, 5, 6, 5, 16, 5, 16, 5, 16, 5, 16, 5, 16, 5, 26, 5, 26, 5, 26, 5, 26, 5, 26, 5, 36, 5, 36, 5, 36, 5, 36, 5, 36, 5, 46, 5, 46, 5, 46, 5, 46, 5, 46, 5, 56, 5, 56, 5, 56, 5, 56, 5, 56, 5, 66, 5, 66, 5, 66, 5, 66, 5, 66]
    }
)

x = non_iid_df["X"]
y = non_iid_df["Y"]

sps_xi_obj = sps.chatterjeexi(
    x=np.asarray(x).astype(np.float64),
    y=np.asarray(y).astype(np.float64),
    nan_policy="omit",
)
sps_xi_cor, sps_xi_p = float(sps_xi_obj.statistic), float(sps_xi_obj.pvalue)
print(
    f"Scipy (version {spv}) with Numpy (version {np.__version__}) ran Chatterjee's Xi and got a corr={round(sps_xi_cor, 5)} w/ P-val={round(sps_xi_p, 5)}\n"
)
```

### Error message

```shell
Scipy (version 1.16.0) with Numpy (version 1.26.4) ran Chatterjee's Xi and got a corr=-0.29444 w/ P-val=0.99953
---------------------
Scipy (version 1.16.0) with Numpy (version 2.0.0) ran Chatterjee's Xi and got a corr=-0.51667 w/ P-val=1.0
```

### SciPy/NumPy/Python version and system information

```shell
sys.version_info(major=3, minor=12, micro=4, releaselevel='final', serial=0)

Build Dependencies:
  blas:
    detection method: pkgconfig

title: DOC: interpolate: RegularGridInterpolator with cubic interpolation : discuss `solver_options` better
body:
### Describe your issue.

The interpolating function returned when using the RegularGridInterpolator with cubic and quintic interpolation is not valid with the default solver (scipy.sparse.linalg.gcrotmk). Changing the solver to scipy.sparse.linalg.spsolve appears to fix the bug. The failure of the default solver is correlated to the presence of a large mean value in the data which is being fit.

In the code below, commenting/uncommenting the solver argument will show how the bug is related to the chosen solver. Modifying the shift and scale values to large/small values will show how the bug is related to the mean value of the data and not the scale/range of the data.


### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy.interpolate import RegularGridInterpolator
from scipy.sparse.linalg import spsolve

shift = 1000 #1000000000
scale = 1

# 1. Define the 3D Gaussian function
def gaussian_3d(x, y, z, x0=0.0, y0=0.0, z0=0.0, sigma=1.0):
    return scale*np.exp(-((x - x0)**2 + (y - y0)**2 + (z - z0)**2) / (2 * sigma**2))+shift

# 2. Create regular 3D grid
x = np.linspace(-3, 3, 5)
y = np.linspace(-3, 3, 5)
z = np.linspace(-3, 3, 5)
X, Y, Z = np.meshgrid(x, y, z, indexing='ij')
F = gaussian_3d(X, Y, Z)

# 3. Create the interpolator (cubic interpolation)
interpolator = RegularGridInterpolator(
    (x, y, z), 
    F,
    method='cubic',
    #solver=spsolve #default solver failsb but spsolve appears ok.
)

# 4. Define 1D slice along x-axis at y=0, z=0
x_dense = np.linspace(-3, 3, 500)
y_fixed = np.zeros_like(x_dense)
z_fixed = np.zeros_like(x_dense)
points = np.vstack([x_dense, y_fixed, z_fixed]).T  # shape (N, 3)

# 5. Interpolate and compute ground truth
f_interp = interpolator(points)
f_true = gaussian_3d(x_dense, y_fixed, z_fixed)

# 6. Plot the 1D slice
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
plt.plot(x_dense, f_true, label='3D Gaussian (y=0, z=0)', linewidth=2)
plt.plot(x_dense, f_interp, '--', label='Cubic Interpolation', linewidth=2)
plt.scatter(x, F[:,2,2])
plt.plot()
plt.ylim([shift-0.25, shift+scale+0.25])

plt.xlabel('x')
plt.ylabel('f(x)')
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### Error message

```shell
No error messages appear with this bug.
```

title: BUG: stats: ppf of 0 returns a value outside the distribution support
body:
### Describe your issue.

I've found a case where passing in a quantile of 0 to the ppf of a distribution is returning a value that is outside the distribution support, which is improper behavior. This should either return nan or the low end of the distribution. I've not dug into this deeply to figure out if it's isolated to randint, discrete distributions, or all distributions.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
from scipy.stats import randint
randint.ppf(0, low=1, high=5)
# np.float64(0.0)  # Not within distribution support!
randint.ppf(1e-16, low=1, high=5)
# np.float64(1.0)
randint.ppf(-1e-16, low=1, high=5)
# np.float64(nan)

# The result with a different lower end shows that it's rounding down to the nearest integer
randint.ppf(0, low=10, high=50)
# np.float64(9.0)
```

title: DOC: interpolate: NearestNDInterpolator works with multi-dimensional arrays but docs suggest otherwise
body:
### Issue with current documentation:

Currently, the [docs](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/de2e92529ca53ae7f67f493ce88ac086348d285b/scipy/interpolate/_ndgriddata.py#L31) for `NearestNDInterpolator` indicate that the `y` array of values should have shape `(npoints, )` and be a 1-D ndarray, but in fact any multi-dimensional array with shape `(npoints, ...)` will work.

For instance, here's a snippet that works just fine on `scipy==1.15.2`:

```python
from scipy.interpolate import NearestNDInterpolator
import numpy as np
x = np.array([[0., 0.], [1., 0.]])
y = np.array([[1., 0.], [0., 1.]])
interp = NearestNDInterpolator(x, y)
res = interp(x)
```

Printing `res` will correctly show:

```shell
array([[1., 0.],
       [0., 1.]])
```

### Idea or request for content:

Unless I'm missing something, the docs should probably be updated to reflect the current behavior:

```python
    """Nearest-neighbor interpolator in N > 1 dimensions.

    ...

    Parameters
    ----------
    x : (npoints, ndims) 2-D ndarray of floats
        Data point coordinates.
    y : (npoints, ...) N-D array of floats or complex
        Data values.
        The length of `y` along the first axis must be equal to
        the length of `x`.

    ...

    """
```

### Additional context (e.g. screenshots, GIFs)

_No response_

title: BUG: (interpolate) _dierckx.data_matrix gives ValueError if input arrays are not C contiguous

### Describe your issue.
Hello Friends --

Got the exception `ValueError: Expected a 1-dim C contiguous array  of dtype = 12`.  

Then went to debug and found that i can get my code to run if the input arrays are not copies.  

But also that it consistently raises exception when the input arrays are views of another.  

The example below is taken from the documentation of `generate_knots`.  https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.interpolate.generate_knots.html

I added one line which makes a copy of the input arrays to demonstrate the error. 
`x, y = np.c_[x, y].T`

-- Thanks!

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy.interpolate import make_lsq_spline, generate_knots
rng = np.random.default_rng()
x = np.linspace(-3, 3, 50)
y = np.exp(-x**2) + 0.1 * rng.standard_normal(size=50)



x, y = np.c_[x, y].T ### THIS LINE WILL MAKE VALUEERROR BELOW


knots = list(generate_knots(x, y, s=1e-10))
for t in knots[::3]:

title: BUG: signal.windows.taylor: output is NaN for nbar >= 2
body:
### Describe your issue.

## Summary
The `scipy.signal.windows.taylor` function consistently produces NaN values when `nbar≥2` for any window length, making it unusable for practical applications. Only `nbar=1` works but degenerates to a rectangular window.

## Bug Description
When calling `taylor(N, nbar, sll)` with `nbar≥2`, the function generates a RuntimeWarning and returns an array containing NaN values:
Additional Observations
Only nbar=1 works but produces rectangular windows (all values ≈ 1.0)
All other window functions work fine (Blackman, Hamming, Kaiser, etc.)
The issue appears systematic - tested lengths from 32 to 256, all fail with nbar≥2
Warning points to line 1821 in _windows.py: A = np.arccosh(B) / np.pi

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
RuntimeWarning: invalid value encountered in arccosh
A = np.arccosh(B) / np.pi
## Reproduction Code

import numpy as np
from scipy.signal.windows import taylor

# Test various parameters
test_cases = [
    (32, 2, -10),
    (64, 2, -15), 
    (128, 3, -20),
    (226, 4, -25),
    (256, 2, -30)
]

for N, nbar, sll in test_cases:
    try:
        win = taylor(N, nbar=nbar, sll=sll, norm=True)
        has_nan = np.any(np.isnan(win))
        has_inf = np.any(np.isinf(win))
        
        print(f"N={N}, nbar={nbar}, sll={sll}: NaN={has_nan}, Inf={has_inf}")
        if has_nan or has_inf:
            print(f"  First few values: {win[:5]}")
            
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"N={N}, nbar={nbar}, sll={sll}: Exception - {e}")
```

### Error message

N=64, nbar=2, sll=-15: NaN=True, Inf=False

title: QUERY: interpolate: Are tck and degrees part of BivariateSpline's public API?
body:
Within BivariateSpline's documentation, there is a note saying this:

>This class is meant to be subclassed, not instantiated directly.

When we say that, do we mean, "this class is meant to be subclassed by SciPy developers, and we might make breaking changes to it?" Or do we mean, "this class is meant to be subclassed by external users?"

If it's the latter, it seems like its quite difficult to write any kind of useful subclass without either reimplementing `__call__`, or setting the self.tck/self.degrees attributes. Are those part of BivariateSpline's public API? Those attributes appear to have existed substantially unchanged since SciPy 0.6.0.

I looked on GitHub to see how many people are sub-classing this. The only example I could find, aside from vendored code, was [this one](https://github.com/brainvisa/sulci-nonfree/blob/cb2e0ebedccd41758cb647984e12245ce3b53996/pysigraph/bin/siDisplayGraph.py#L437). Curiously, it seems to be much more common to subclass RectBivariateSpline.

* [GitHub search for subclassing any subclass of BivariateSpline](https://github.com/search?q=%2Fclass+.*%5C%28.*BivariateSpline%5C%29%2F+NOT+path%3A**%2Ffitpack2.py+NOT+path%3A**%2F_fitpack2.py+language%3APython&type=code)
* [GitHub search for subclassing BivariateSpline directly](https://github.com/search?q=%2Fclass+.*%5C%28BivariateSpline%5C%29%2F+NOT+path%3A**%2Ffitpack2.py+NOT+path%3A**%2F_fitpack2.py+language%3APython&type=code)

title: BUG: Possible Issue with class RectBivariateSpline(BivariateSpline) in the _fitpack2.py
body:
### Describe your issue.

Hello,

I apologize if this is my misunderstanding, but in the __init__ function under the class RectBivariateSpline(BivariateSpline), there are checks for x and y starting on line 1585 as follows:

        if not np.all(diff(x) > 0.0):
            raise ValueError('x must be strictly increasing')
        if not np.all(diff(y) > 0.0):
            raise ValueError('y must be strictly increasing')

Would this not be a contradiction? Because, if x is increasing then np.all(diff(x) > 0.0) == True, but by using the not operator, it would become False, thus leading to the error. Am I wrong or am I just missing something?

title: BUG: setting with `sparse.xxx_{matrix,array}` 2D singleton
body:
### Describe your issue.

I would like to be able to set on a sparse matrix/array with a singleton-sparse-array the same way I can with a singleton dense array. 

The origin of this issue lies in setting in `dask` where the setter and the set-ee are identically typed.

I think doing something like 
```python
if issparse(x):
    x = x.toarray()
else:
    x = np.asarray(x, dtype=self.dtype)
```
instead of 
https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/abb598d7e5634cddb903a0ad7b42f8b57c895719/scipy/sparse/_index.py#L165
is sufficient and indeed, this check already exists in the 1d case:
https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/abb598d7e5634cddb903a0ad7b42f8b57c895719/scipy/sparse/_index.py#L131-L134

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np, scipy.sparse as sp

arr = sp.csr_matrix([[2, 3], [4, 5]])
arr[0, 0] = sp.csr_matrix([[0]])
```

title: Why does `special_cyl_bessel_k_int` (and therefore `scipy.special.kn`) use `cyl_bessel_k` and not `cephes::kn`?
body:
I noticed an odd inconsistency:
- The function `scipy.special.yn` [calls into `xsf::cephes::yn`](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/v1.16.0/scipy/special/xsf_wrappers.cpp#L337) through `cephes_yn_wrap`.
- The function `scipy.special.kn` [calls into `xsf::cyl_bessel_k`](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/v1.16.0/scipy/special/xsf_wrappers.cpp#L522) through `special_cyl_bessel_k_int`, instead of `xsf::cephes::kn`.

This seems to have been introduced in bfc1a81e8443ebf0128d1e580b79eaf30ea4e3f7, so I hope it’s not too bothersome to ping @izaid.
Is there a reason that `xsf::cephes::kn` was avoided, or was this an oversight?

title: BUG: stats.sampling: segfault in various classes on osx-arm64
body:
### Describe your issue.

The following scipy.stats.sampling classes all crash with a segmentation fault on macOS when instantiated with the documented example code:
- NumericalInverseHermite
- NumericalInversePolynomial
- TransformedDensityRejection
- FastGeneratorInversion

Each class was used _exactly_ as in the SciPy documentation or simple minimal‐example, without any modifications.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
# 1. NumericalInverseHermite
from scipy.stats.sampling import NumericalInverseHermite
from scipy.special import ndtr
import numpy as np


class StandardNormal:
    def pdf(self, x):
       return 1/np.sqrt(2*np.pi) * np.exp(-x**2 / 2)
    def cdf(self, x):
       return ndtr(x)

dist = StandardNormal()
urng = np.random.default_rng()
rng = NumericalInverseHermite(dist, random_state=urng)
# → segmentation fault


# 2. NumericalInversePolynomial
from scipy.stats.sampling import NumericalInversePolynomial
import numpy as np


class StandardNormal:
   def pdf(self, x):
       return np.exp(-0.5 * x*x)

dist = StandardNormal()
urng = np.random.default_rng()
rng = NumericalInversePolynomial(dist, random_state=urng)
# → segmentation fault


# 3. TransformedDensityRejection
from scipy.stats.sampling import TransformedDensityRejection
import numpy as np


urng = np.random.default_rng()
class MyDist:
    def pdf(self, x):
        return 1-x*x
    def dpdf(self, x):
        return -2*x

dist = MyDist()
rng = TransformedDensityRejection(dist, domain=(-1, 1),
                                  random_state=urng)
# → segmentation fault


# 4. FastGeneratorInversion
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats
from scipy.stats.sampling import FastGeneratorInversion


gamma_frozen = stats.gamma(1.5)
gamma_dist = FastGeneratorInversion(gamma_frozen)
# → segmentation fault
```

title: ENH: spatial.transform: array API standard support tracker
body:
## Summary
Substantial effort has recently gone into making the `spatial.transform` module fully array API compatible. This issue intends to give an overview over all PRs and potential follow-ups to organize efforts and give reviewers a clearer idea where we stand.

Related issues: #18867, #22500

## List of past and upcoming PRs

- [x] #22939
- [x] #22972
- [x] #23198
- [x] #23193
- [x] #23249
- [x] #23424
- [x] #23425

## Optional extension
The new backends make it easier to add support for arbitrary leading dimensions. `RigidTransform` and `Rotation` would follow standard broadcasting rules. This would be a very nice feature to have, but has not been seriously considered so far. See #23437 for more info

- [x] #23467 
- [x]  #23675
- [x] #23632 
- [x] #23690
- [x] #23695

## Improved API for `identity`, `random`, `mean`
@scottshambaugh proposed to update the API for `identity`, `random` and `mean`, now that `Rotation` and `RigidTransform` can be N-dimensional arrays.

- [x] `Rotation.identity`: #23869
- [x] `RigidTransform.identity`: #23875
- [x] `Rotation.random`: #23869
- [x] `Rotation.mean`: #23828
- [x] `RigidTransform.mean`: #23718
- [x] `RigidTransform.mean`: #23933


## Benchmarks
Below you can find the current benchmarks for `Rotation` and `RigidTransform` on different frameworks.

- `Rotation`: [rotation_benchmark.pdf](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/22265227/rotation_benchmark.pdf)
- `RigidTransform`: [rigid_transform_benchmark.pdf](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/22541264/tf_benchmark.pdf)

@lucascolley @crusaderky @scottshambaugh

title: BUG: interpolate: `test_bispev_integer_overflow` crashes for ILP64 build on macOS
body:
### Describe your issue.

After adding ILP64 support to Accelerate, I found that `test_bispev_integer_overflow` is the one test that fails. The `_dfitpack` code it calls doesn't even use BLAS, so this crash will also show up for other ILP64 builds on macOS, and possibly on other platforms as well.

This test is a regression test for gh-12471, and that issue was closed after multiple attempts in gh-16649. The PRs that superseded, gh-16084 and gh-12473, contain more relevant discussion. The questions about "will this `F_INT` work when ILP64 is brought back" in there were justified it looks like.

For now I'll just skip the test on Accelerate ILP64, but it warrants fixing properly or working around structurally by just rejecting trying to allocate arrays larger than `2**31`.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
% python -m pytest scipy/interpolate/tests/test_fitpack.py -k integer_overflow

title: ENH: spatial.geometric_slerp: allow for extrapolation
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

Currently, the [geometric_slerp](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.spatial.geometric_slerp.html) function restricts the interpolation parameter to be within 0 to 1. Is there a specific reason for this? To me, it seems like the implementation would also allow for values outside of this range. This would allow linear extrapolation on a sphere as well, instead of only interpolation.

### Describe the solution you'd like.

Maybe, the constrain in [this line](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/7663bce95938d2db153546f43d360a7e6d4edaed/scipy/spatial/_geometric_slerp.py#L229) can be removed.

### Describe alternatives you've considered.

_No response_

### Additional context (e.g. screenshots, GIFs)

_No response_

title: BUG: stats.pmean: bad numerical behavior for small but finite p
body:
### Describe your issue.

`pmean` suffers from poor numerical behavior when `p` is tiny but nonzero. `p=0` is handled correctly, because in that case, the calculation is equivalent to the geometric mean.

I don't know if anyone would ever use `pmean` with small enough values where this behavior would be of concern. I only ran into this while looking into https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/23111.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
In [59]: from scipy import stats

In [60]: from mpsci.stats import pmean as mp_pmean

In [61]: from mpmath import mp

In [62]: mp.dps = 80

In [63]: x = np.array([50.0, 100.0, 300.0, 70.0, 25.0, 100.0, 2.0])

In [64]: stats.pmean(x, 0).item()  # Verify correct behavior with p=0.
Out[64]: 47.239845864458545

In [65]: float(mp_pmean(x, 0))
Out[65]: 47.239845864458545

In [66]: stats.pmean(x, 0.125).item()  # Verify correct behavior with p=0.125
Out[66]: 53.57867853658302

In [67]: float(mp_pmean(x, 0.125))
Out[67]: 53.57867853658306

In [68]: p = 2e-16  # Ridiculously small value of p (error increases as p decreases)

In [69]: stats.pmean(x, p).item()  # Completely wrong
Out[69]: 27.9570407726259

In [70]: float(mp_pmean(x, p))  # The expected value.
Out[70]: 47.23984586445856

In [71]: p = 1e-13  # Bigger, but still extremely small.

In [72]: stats.pmean(x, p).item()  # Only about 3 digits correct.
Out[72]: 47.21418331895157

In [73]: float(mp_pmean(x, p))
Out[73]: 47.239845864463646
```

title: ENH: stats.kendall_tau: MergeSort to achieve O(n log n) complexity
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

The naive computation of concordant pairs has $O(n^2)$ complexity, which makes this computation prohibitive for large data sets (millions). This is what scipy currently implements, see https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/main/scipy/stats/_stats.pyx#L84

### Describe the solution you'd like.

As described in the Wikipedia, one can compute the number of concordant pairs using MergeSort in O(n log n)
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kendall_rank_correlation_coefficient#Algorithms, original source https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/01621459.1966.10480879
which is a huge advantage, obviously, for large datasets.

### Describe alternatives you've considered.

I don't know of alternatives.

### Additional context (e.g. screenshots, GIFs)

_No response_

title: BUG: stats.qmc.Sobol: stuck in infinite loop in low_0_bit after generating 2**32 samples
body:
### Describe your issue.

After generating 2**32 samples, it appears that low_0_bit can get stuck in an infinite loop.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
from scipy.stats import qmc
import math


def run():
    batch_size = 2 ** 26
    samples = 2 ** 33
    n_batches = samples // batch_size
    sampler = qmc.Sobol(d=2, bits=64)
    samples = 0

    for i in range(n_batches):
        print(f"at step {i}")
        qmc_samples = sampler.random(n=batch_size)
        samples += len(qmc_samples)
        print(f"generated 2**{math.log2(samples):.1f} samples")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    run()
```

### Error message

```shell
at step 0
generated 2**26.0 samples
at step 1
generated 2**27.0 samples
at step 2
generated 2**27.6 samples
at step 3
generated 2**28.0 samples
at step 4
generated 2**28.3 samples
at step 5
generated 2**28.6 samples

generated 2**28.8 samples
at step 7
generated 2**29.0 samples
at step 8
generated 2**29.2 samples
at step 9
generated 2**29.3 samples
at step 10

at step 11

```


### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import tracemalloc

import numpy as np
import scipy.signal

tracemalloc.start()

array = np.random.randn(1000).astype(float)

for _ in range(50_000):
    frequencies, Pxx = scipy.signal.periodogram(
        array,
        1e12,
        window="hamming",
        detrend="linear",
    )

snapshot = tracemalloc.take_snapshot()
top_stats = snapshot.statistics("lineno")
print("[ Top 3 memory allocations ]")
for stat in top_stats[:3]:
    print(stat)
```

title: BUG: stats: scipy 1.16 causes a large speed regression in mwaskom/seaborn `tests/test_statistics.py::TestKDE::test_bivariate_cumulative`
BUG: Error constructing sparse matrix with indices larger than 32 bits from smaller matrices, using sparse.block_diag()
body:
### Describe your issue.

Very similar case to #14623 - using a smaller matrix as input for block_diag in order to construct a large sparse matrix results in integer overflow. The lines in question are https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/main/scipy/sparse/_construct.py#L1171 (1177 would cause the same exception)
```Python
row.append(a.row + r_idx)
col.append(a.col + c_idx)
```
A solution similar to #14626 is ideal but the required dimensions / dtype can't be determined in advance, and I'm not sure what the best practice would be for a lightweight check.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
from scipy import sparse

n = 50000 # Greater than sqrt(2^31)
A = sparse.coo_array((n,n)).tocsr()

# n x n**2 matrix
B = sparse.block_diag(A)
```

### Error message

```shell
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File ".../block_array.py", line 7, in <module>
    B = sparse.block_diag(A)
        ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File ".../.env/dd/lib/python3.11/site-packages/scipy/sparse/_construct.py", line 1123, in block_diag
    col.append(a.col + c_idx)
               ~~~~~~^~~~~~~
OverflowError: Python integer 2147500000 out of bounds for int32
```

title: BUG: scipy.signal.square ignores input dtype due to incorrect dtype check
body:
### Describe your issue.

The square function in scipy.signal always returns an output of dtype float64, even when the input t is of a different float type (e.g. float32), due to an incorrect type check.

code snippet with error:

if t.dtype.char in ['fFdD']:
    ytype = t.dtype.char
else:
    ytype = 'd'

This line always fails because ['fFdD'] is a list containing a single string, not a list of characters.


### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
from scipy.signal import square

t = np.linspace(0, 1, 100, dtype=np.float32)
y = square(2 * np.pi * t)
print(f'input type is: {t.dtype}')  # float32
print(f'output type is {y.dtype}')  # always float64  as bug exists
```

### Error message

```shell
input type is: float32
output type is float64
```

title: BUG: Discrete time TransferFunction objects calculate wrong phase response in recursive filters due to internal function bugs
body:
### Describe your issue.

When comparing the frequency response of a simple dlti system I realize that the phase response were all NaN's. Inspecting the code, I found two problems: one that was not important in the final result but was incorrect (I first thought that this was the problem), and the second, was caused by an "unprotected" division by zero, this was the real problem of the NaN phase calculation. 

The unprotected division were found at:

_filter_design.py 482:

        h = (npp_polyval(zm1, b, tensor=False) /
             (npp_polyval(zm1, a, tensor=False) ) )

solution:

        h = (npp_polyval(zm1, b, tensor=False) /
            (npp_polyval(zm1, a, tensor=False)+ np.finfo(float).tiny ) )


Also a warning could be omitted if:

_ltisys.py 3594:
    
    mag = 20.0 * np.log10(abs(y))
    
solution:

    mag = 20.0 * np.log10(abs(y) + np.finfo(float).tiny )


Other more important problem that I found is related to coefficient conversion, when translating T(z) = P(z)/Q(z) into P(z^-1)/Q(z^-1), in:

_ltisys.py: 

function "_z_to_zinv" is completely wrong IMHO

This would be my version, with documentation, and three cases for testing.

    @staticmethod
    def _z_to_zinv(num, den):
        r"""
        Convert a digital transfer function T(z) = P(z)/Q(z) to T(z⁻¹), 
        removing common delays.
    
        This function rewrites a discrete-time transfer function expressed in terms
        of positive powers of ``z`` into one expressed in terms of negative powers
        ``z⁻¹``. That is, it transforms a transfer function of the form
    
    
        Parameters
        ----------
        num : array_like
            Numerator coefficients of the transfer function in descending powers
            of ``z``. For example, ``z^2 - 1`` is represented as ``[1, 0, -1]``.
        den : array_like
            Denominator coefficients of the transfer function in descending powers
            of ``z``.
    
        Returns
        -------
        num_zinv : ndarray
            Numerator coefficients of the transfer function in ascending powers of
            ``z⁻¹``, with common delays removed.
        den_zinv : ndarray
            Denominator coefficients of the transfer function in ascending powers of
            ``z⁻¹``, with common delays removed.
    
        Examples
        --------
        Case 1: Numerator has more trailing zeros than denominator
        (e.g., numerator divisible by higher power of z⁻¹):
    
        >>> num = [1, 0, 0, -1]     # z³ - 1
        >>> den = [1, -1, 0, 0]     # z³ - z²
        >>> z_to_zinv_trim(num, den)
        (array([ 1,  0,  0, -1]), array([ 1, -1]))  #  (1 - z⁻³) (1 - z⁻¹) 
    
        Case 2: Denominator has more trailing zeros than numerator:
    
        >>> num = [1, 0, 0]      # z² 
        >>> den = [1, 0, -1]     # z² - 1
        >>> z_to_zinv_trim(num, den)
        (array([ 1]), array([ 1, 0, -1]))  #  (1 ) (1 - z⁻²) 
    
        Case 3: Both numerator and denominator have the same number of trailing zeros:
    
        >>> num = [1, 1]     # z + 1
        >>> den = [1, -1]    # z - 1
        >>> z_to_zinv_trim(num, den)
        (array([ 1,  1]), array([ 1, -1])) #  (1 + z⁻¹) (1 - z⁻¹) 
        """
    
        num_rev = num[::-1]
        den_rev = den[::-1]
    
        # Buscar el menor grado no nulo en z^-1 (o sea, posición del primer coef ≠ 0)
        deg_num = np.argmax(num_rev != 0)
        deg_den = np.argmax(den_rev != 0)
    
        # Normalizar al mínimo grado común
        
        if deg_num > deg_den:

            num_zinv = np.trim_zeros(np.asarray(num), 'b')
            den_zinv = den

        elif deg_num < deg_den:

            num_zinv = num
            den_zinv = np.trim_zeros(np.asarray(den), 'b')
        
        else:

            num_zinv = num
            den_zinv = den
    
        return num_zinv, den_zinv






### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
import scipy.signal as sig
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Orden 5
N = 5
fs = 2

# Promediador de orden N
# TransferFunction los asume en potencias decrecientes
num = 1/(N)* np.ones(N)
den = np.zeros(N)
den[0] = 1

my_df = sig.TransferFunction(num, den, dt=1/fs)
w, mod, pha1 = my_df.bode(512)


# Promediador de orden N recursivo
# TransferFunction los asume en potencias decrecientes
num_R = 1/N*np.concatenate([[1], np.zeros(N-1),[-1]])
den_R = np.zeros(N+1)
den_R[:2] = np.array([1, -1]) 

my_df_R = sig.TransferFunction(num_R, den_R, dt=1/fs)

w, mod, pha2 = my_df_R.bode(512)

# Promediador de orden N recursivo
# ordenado en potencias z⁻¹
num_zinv = 1/N*np.concatenate([[1], np.zeros(N-1),[-1]])
den_zinv = np.array([1, -1]) 

w, h = sig.freqz(num_zinv, den_zinv, worN=512)

pha_zinv = np.angle(h)

plt.figure(1)
plt.clf()
plt.plot(w, pha1/180*np.pi, 'o:')
plt.plot(w, pha2/180*np.pi, 'x:')
plt.plot(w, np.unwrap(pha_zinv), ':')
```

pha2 and pha_unw_zinv variables are all nan.
```

title: BUG: optimize.nnls: memory leak
body:
### Describe your issue.

When running a scipy-using code base under asan, asan reported a memory leak here: https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blame/93af8eeed5b33ea6b1d0bbce62eb877b5c156d3d/scipy/optimize/__slsqp.h#L186

From inspecting the code, it is clearly incorrect. The output of `realloc()` is passed only to `PyArray_SimpleNewFromData`, but that https://numpy.org/devdocs/reference/c-api/array.html#c.PyArray_SimpleNewFromData does nothing to free the block it is passed.

title: BUG: optimize: missing error checking for slsqp inputs
body:
### Describe your issue.

When trying to upgrade to SciPy 1.16 in our code base, I saw this error:

```
...
File ".../scipy/optimize/_minimize.py", line 796, in minimize
    res = _minimize_slsqp(fun, x0, args, jac, bounds,
          ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File ".../scipy/optimize/_slsqp_py.py", line 517, in _minimize_slsqp
    slsqp(state_dict, fx, g, C, d, x, mult, xl, xu, buffer, indices)
SystemError: <built-in function slsqp> returned a result with an exception set
```

When building with Py_DEBUG mode, this became:
```
...
Fatal Python error: _Py_CheckFunctionResult: a function returned a result with an exception set
Python runtime state: initialized
TypeError: 'float' object cannot be interpreted as an integer
```

I traced the problem in a debugger to this line of code:

https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/93af8eeed5b33ea6b1d0bbce62eb877b5c156d3d/scipy/optimize/__slsqp.h#L262

If `PyLong_AsLong` fails, e.g., because the input was a python float, we need to return NULL or we'll get the above error from the python interpreter.

I presume the same problem exists here also: https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/93af8eeed5b33ea6b1d0bbce62eb877b5c156d3d/scipy/optimize/__slsqp.h#L255

@ilayn (https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/22524)

title: DOC: interpolate.RbfInterpolator: incorrect equation in Notes
body:
### Issue with current documentation:

In the RBFInterpolator docs, it uses the equation

    f(x) = K(x, y) a + P(x) b,

to describe the K matrix that is used at prediction time.

However, it uses the equation

    (K(y, y) + \\lambda I) a + P(y) b = d

to describe the K matrix that is used for fitting.

I think "K(y, y)" is incorrect. Based on this part of the source code, it appears that K(x, y) is used for both fitting and predicting the RbfInterpolator.

https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/93af8eeed5b33ea6b1d0bbce62eb877b5c156d3d/scipy/interpolate/_rbfinterp_pythran.py#L63-L68

### Idea or request for content:

Replace K(y, y) with K(x, y)

### Additional context (e.g. screenshots, GIFs)

https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.interpolate.RBFInterpolator.html

title: ENH: spatial.transform: Add broadcasting support
body:
# Feature description

At the moment, `Rotation` and `RigidTransform` only accept either single rotations/transforms, or an array of rotations/transforms. This limitation stems from the original Cython implementation that did not use broadcasting. With the newly introduced generic backends which already use broadcasting internally, it is possible to remove this restriction and apply regular broadcasting logic (see #23249 and #23425). 

This  would enable users to use generically shaped rotations, i.e. a batch of 3x2 quaternions.

# Design
Broadcasting rules are well established and offer a straightforward way of determining the result each operation should have. Ideally, we state that all operations in `Rotation`/`RigidTransform` follow standard broadcasting rules. This would remove the arbitrary limitation to a list of rotations/transforms and would make using `Rotation`/`RigidTransform` feel a bit more like dealing with an array wrapper around quaternions/matrices.

## Implementation considerations
Overall, the scope and intended result are clearly defined by building on broadcasting rules. However, the implementation requires some consideration.

### Fully support broadcasting in the backends
The xp backends already make heavy use of broadcasting to produce the correct output shapes without distinction of cases (what a happy coincidence!). However, some functions don't have full broadcasting support yet. These would need to be addressed.

### numpy handling
We currently use the Cython backend for single rotations and lists of rotations. We cannot open this up to arbitrary shapes because of Cython type restrictions. I do not recommend dropping the Cython backend, as it provides speedups for some of the most common cases (single Rotation or smaller lists of Rotations). However, for tensors we could use the xp backends. Users requiring batches with multiple dimensions will probably work with larger data sizes anyways, where the xp backends sometimes become more efficient than the Cython backend. In addition, this minimizes maintenance load. The xp backends are already tested.

### Changes to functions
Some functions (e.g. `mean` or groups) would greatly benefit from additional functionality or require additional parameters for clarification. For example, `mean` should probably take an axis argument if broadcasting is possible.

## Alternative options

We could also expose the functional array API backends to users which use broadcasting for most implementations. However, these functions rely on the frontend for some sanity checks (e.g. they assume input quaternions are valid and normalized), are not documented at the moment and thus are less safe for users to use.


@lucascolley @scottshambaugh @crusaderky

title: ENH: Vectorized spline interpolation for both x and y
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

I need to perform a large number of short splines, all with the same number of points, but different x and y values. E.g. x_in[1000000,4], y_in[1000000,4], which I want to be able to interpolate using e.g. x_out[1000000,5] to get y_out[1000000,5]. Scipy's spline interface supports vectorization for y, but requires x to be the same for all (so for my example, it would require x_in[4] and x_out[5]).

### Describe the solution you'd like.

Add support for x.ndim > 1 in the spline functions.

### Describe alternatives you've considered.

* A slow loop in python
* Writing an extension module in a low-level language
* Manual vectorized calculation of spline coefficients (or maybe just a polynomial) in numpy

title: CI: _lib: `test_pyyaml_not_found` not automatically detected as thread-unsafe
body:
### Issue summary

In PR #23426, the test `scipy/_lib/tests/test_config.py::TestSciPyConfigs::test_pyyaml_not_found` is failing in the free-threaded build when run in parallel.

This test is failing because it uses `@unittest.mock.patch`, which is thread-unsafe if multiple threads are modifying the same global value.

### Why the `thread_unsafe` marker was removed

This test used to be marked with `@pytest.mark.thread_unsafe`, until PR #23294 removed it. This marker was removed because because pytest-run-parallel can detect the thread-unsafe functionality. This was true in pytest-run-parallel version 0.4.4, but not true in version 0.5.0, because pytest-run-parallel can no longer parse the function correctly.

The problem is that this function contains an indented decorator, which is not parsed correctly.

```
    @patch("scipy.__config__._check_pyyaml")
    def test_pyyaml_not_found(self, mock_yaml_importer):
        mock_yaml_importer.side_effect = ModuleNotFoundError()
        with pytest.warns(UserWarning):
            scipy.show_config()
```

See Quansight-Labs/pytest-run-parallel#108 for more detail.

Until that is fixed, the short term solution is to re-add `@pytest.mark.thread_unsafe` here.

title: DOC/ENH: signal.get_window: different default for symmetric/periodic than individual window functions
body:
### Issue with current documentation:

The `scipy.signal.windows.get_window` function ([doc](https://scipy.github.io/devdocs/reference/generated/scipy.signal.windows.get_window.html)) has a parameter "fftbins". It changes the generated window between being symmetric and periodic. The default is `True`, meaning to create a **periodic** window.

However, it appears that all of the individual window functions such as [`scipy.signal.windows.hann`](https://scipy.github.io/devdocs/reference/generated/scipy.signal.windows.hann.html) instead have a parameter "sym". Its default value is also `True`, but in this case it means to create a **symmetric** window (sym for symmetric).

Both conventions are acceptable, but the problem arises when these functions, which one might assume behave identically, just with different API, use a different convention and different default.

I ran into this footgun myself where I assumed that I was getting symmtric window from `get_window`, just like I read was the default for the individual window functions. This resulted in errors in my application that I assumed had to be coming from somewhere else, or was an intrinsic property of the windows themselves. I only realized this when I tried to pass a value to `sym` via the tuple, and got an error about the parameter being set multiple times.

### Idea or request for content:

Ideally these functions would use the same convention, or at least the same default. Changing that at this point would maybe be more trouble than it's worth, though.

I think the `get_window` function's documentation should have a prominent warning about this discrepancy. Something like

> [!WARNING]
> Unlike the individual window functions under `scipy.signal.windows`, this function generates a **periodic** window by default. The meaning of the `fftbins` parameter is opposite from the `sym` parameter on the individual window functions.

If this is considered a big enough DX issue, one path forward might be to deprecate `get_window` and replace it with a different name such as `make_window` (subject to bikeshedding).

### Additional context (e.g. screenshots, GIFs)

_No response_

title: ENH: special: `hyp2f1(a, b, c, z)` implementation for complex arguments (a, b)
body: ### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe. The current implementation of `scipy.special.hyp2f1(a, b, c, z)` seems to work for real-valued arguments (a, b, c) only. This function is useful in several fields (like atomic cross-section calculations for bremsttrahlung radiation) with complex arguments. For an example of an application requiring complex arguments see [1], eq (22): [1] [https://journals-aps-org.libproxy.mit.edu/pr/abstract/10.1103/PhysRev.183.90](https://journals-aps-org.libproxy.mit.edu/pr/abstract/10.1103/PhysRev.183.90) ### Describe the solution you'd like. An implementation of this function with complex arguments is possible and has been proposed for example in: [2] [https://arxiv.org/pdf/0708.0116](https://arxiv.org/pdf/0708.0116) It is also implemented here: [3] https://mpmath.readthedocs.io/en/latest/functions/hypergeometric.html More context on hypergeometric function 2F1 with multiple complex variable can be found in: [4] [https://www.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2:190819/FULLTEXT01.pdf](https://www.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2:190819/FULLTEXT01.pdf) This issue probably also pertains to [issue 1561](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/1561), which, if I understood correctly, was leading to the notion that this function should be re-implemented to address some numerical errors, adding the ability to handle complex input arguments would be a useful added feature if that effort in underway. ### Describe alternatives you've considered. Have been looking online for open-source implementations of this but haven't found anything ### Additional context (e.g. screenshots, GIFs)

### Describe your issue.

Saving a `coo_array` with more than 2 dimensions with `scipy.sparse.save_npz` and trying to load it with `load_npz` fails with a `ValueError`:

The loading code seems hardcoded to handle 2D only: https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/0f2342f34cc850e7137a74afd33c6877c1c790e6/scipy/sparse/_matrix_io.py#L162

import numpy as np
import scipy as sp

arr = np.zeros((10, 10, 10))
coo = sp.sparse.coo_array(arr)

sp.sparse.load_npz('test.npz')

title: ENH: Add covariance and error methods to `normal` and similar models
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

Hi,

I need something very small and quick to do fits to a Gaussian distribution, so I am doing:

```python
from scipy.stats import normal

mu, sg = normal.fit(data)
```

and I am very happy that I can do this with minimal disruption to my code. However, given that the data is finite, i do not just need to know the `mu` but also the uncertainities. A `$\mu=1\pm 0.1$` is for sure not the same as a `$\mu=1\pm 1$. And I see that there is no quick way to do this. I would do something like


```python
from scipy.stats import normal

mu, sg = normal.fit(data)     # Same as before
em, es = normal.fit_errors(data) # Clean and minimally disruptive extra functionality
```

where the `fit` method caches the `mu` and `sg` values such that the `fit_errors` method reuses them. In the case of the Gaussian distribution, I can just use the analytical form of the errors, but in other cases e.g. the analytical form is harder to get, it would be better to just have a numerical calculation of the errors. Which I would override with an analytical version whenever possible.

Cheers.

### Describe the solution you'd like.

_No response_

### Describe alternatives you've considered.

_No response_

### Additional context (e.g. screenshots, GIFs)

_No response_

ENH: interpolate.make_smoothing_spline: `_compute_optimal_gcv_parameter` goes wrong
body: ### Describe your issue. [This has been discussed on Stackoverflow](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/79736522/automatic-smoothing-parameters-with-make-smoothing-spline-go-wrong) I would like to determine optimal smoothing parameters for any kind of time series, with mixed units, varying lengths, varying framerates, such as pixel or meter trajectories, radian or degree angles, velocities, etc. I tried **make_smoothing_spline** from scipy.interpolate, but this does not work so well. I've got 2 **normalized** time series (mean 1e-16≈0, std=1). Both curves are almost identical, but smoothing them with make_smoothing_spline gives very different results when lam=None (automatically determined with the GCV criterion). One is correctly smoothed (green), the other is completely flattened (red). I picked out the code of make_smoothing_spline and saw that there is a call to [scipy.interpolate._bsplines._compute_optimal_gcv_parameter(X, wE, y, w)](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/b46531b114017f9817da80a9d6c2b2eaad0a2df2/scipy/interpolate/_bsplines.py#L2397). Calling it on the first curve gives **lam = 4.9e-06**, while the other gives **154.0**. Note that I had similar outcomes with much more dynamic and noisy time series. Should I simply not use normalized data? But then I have the opposite problem, with some data not being filtered at all. And most importantly, why does it behave like so? Is it some sort of numerical precision issue? Any idea how to handle it? --- [Data to reproduce the issue](https://lam.tiiny.site/) ### Reproducing Code Example ```python import pickle import matplotlib.pyplot as plt from scipy.interpolate import title: BUG: signal.lombscargle(precenter=True) modifies input array on 1.15+
body:
### Describe your issue.

With the `signal.lombscargle` rework in 1.15, there has been a silent API change where passing `precenter=True` now leads to in-place modification of one of the input arrays.

In my opinion, this is a breaking API change – and a rather unfortunate one, as it silently changes the behaviour of programs which care about the DC offset of the input data in later use.

Previously, `precenter=True` just led to an extra allocation internally to hold `y - y.mean()`. The `precenter` option has existed with this behaviour since 2015 (released at least in 1.0). It was not explicitly documented as not modifying the input array, but I would argue that unless documented otherwise, this would be surprising behaviour to begin with – one wouldn't expect a routine to modify its input parameters, even more so if the operation isn't even conceptually something that could be done in-place. The current implementation leaks an intermediary product (the offset-subtracted version) into the user's input argument.

Especially since the flag is documented as "legacy"
> Pre-center measurement values by subtracting the mean, if True. This is a legacy parameter and unnecessary if floating_mean is True.

silently changing the behaviour like this just to avoid one allocation in a non-default, now-discouraged code path doesn't seem like a good tradeoff to me. It would probably even be a better user experience to deprecate the option outright and direct the user to pass in `y - y.mean()` directly.

(many thanks to @adammj in #21697 for tracking this down!)


### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
import scipy.signal as ss
x = np.arange(3)
y = np.arange(3, dtype=np.float64)
_pgram = ss.lombscargle(x, y, [1], precenter=True)
print(y)  # Changed to [-1.  0.  1.] now.
```

title: ENH: Slight inaccuracy in lfilter_zi gives different result of filtfilt
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

I compared the results of the function of filtfilt in SciPy, Matlab, GNU Octave, with the same arguments:
```
b = [1., 0., 0.]
a = [1., 1., -6.]
x = [8., 8., 8., 8., 8., 8., 8., 8.]
```
As mentioned in [https://dsp.stackexchange.com/questions/11466/differences-between-python-and-matlab-filtfilt-function](https://dsp.stackexchange.com/questions/11466/differences-between-python-and-matlab-filtfilt-function), the default padding lengths are different. In Matlab's filtfilt, it is ```3*(max(len(a), len(b)) - 1)```, and in SciPy's filtfilt, it is ```3*max(len(a), len(b))```. So I set the padding length to ```3*(max(len(a), len(b)) - 1) = 6``` in SciPy, i.e.
```y = filtfilt(b, a, x, padlen=6)```.
Then the outputs of SciPy and Matlab were the same,
```y = [0.35031103, 0.54860736, 0.48315307, 0.50529341, 0.49807441, 0.5005613, 0.49977262, 0.50005565]```.
To my surprise, GNU Octave gave a different result,
```y = [0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5]```.

I compared the source codes of filtfilt in SciPy and GNU Octave, and found the reason:
In SciPy, the steady state of the filter's step response zi was ```[-1.25, -1.5+2.22044605e-16]```, while in GNU Octave it was ```[-1.25, -1.5]```. In SciPy, zi was given by the function lfilter_zi(b, a) in [line 4827](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/v1.16.1/scipy/signal/_signaltools.py#L4827) of _signaltools.py. If I set zi to ```[-1.25, -1.5]``` after that, then the output was ```y = [0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5]``` which was the same with that of GNU Octave.

### Describe the solution you'd like.

The GNU Octave code in filtfilt.m that calculates si (zi in SciPy) is

<details>
<summary>GNU Octave code (warning: GPL license)</summary>

```
kdc = sum(b) / sum(a);
if (abs(kdc) < inf)
    si = fliplr(cumsum(fliplr(b - kdc * a)));
else
    si = zeros(size(a));
end
si(1) = [];
```

</details>

I wonder if the implementation of the function lfilter_zi(b, a) in SciPy should be improved, and some ideas might be borrowed from GNU Octave.

### Describe alternatives you've considered.

_No response_

### Additional context (e.g. screenshots, GIFs)

_No response_

title: DOC: optimize.differential_evolution: Clarify trial vector recombination - fill_point index is random, not fixed to last parameter
body:
### Issue with current documentation:

The documentation for [scipy.optimize.differential_evolution](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy-1.16.1/reference/generated/scipy.optimize.differential_evolution.html#scipy.optimize.differential_evolution) states:

"The final parameter is always loaded from b' (referring to generating the trial candidate after recombination)."

However, in the implementation (_differential_evolution.py), the fill_point index is actually randomized:

```
def _mutate(self, candidate):
        """Create a trial vector based on a mutation strategy."""
        rng = self.random_number_generator

        if callable(self.strategy):
            return self._mutate_custom(candidate)

        fill_point = rng_integers(rng, self.parameter_count)
        samples = self._select_samples(candidate, 5)

        trial = np.copy(self.population[candidate])

        if self.strategy in ['currenttobest1exp', 'currenttobest1bin']:
            bprime = self.mutation_func(candidate, samples)
        else:
            bprime = self.mutation_func(samples)

        crossovers = rng.uniform(size=self.parameter_count)
        crossovers = crossovers < self.cross_over_probability
        if self.strategy in self._binomial:
            # the last one is always from the bprime vector for binomial
            # If you fill in modulo with a loop you have to set the last one to
            # true. If you don't use a loop then you can have any random entry
            # be True.
            crossovers[fill_point] = True
            trial = np.where(crossovers, bprime, trial)
            return trial
```

This means any parameter index can leveraged, which contradicts what the documentation says. The comments within the self.strategy in self._binomial_case are confusing as well, with one saying "the last one is always from b'" and another saying "if you don't use a loop, then you can have any random entry be True."


### Idea or request for content:

Update the documentation to state: "For binomial crosssover, one random parameter index is always selected from b' for the trial candidate to ensure the trial candidate differs from the parent"

### Additional context (e.g. screenshots, GIFs)

_No response_

title: ENH: zipfian.rvs is slow for certain argument values
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

The scipy.stats.zipfian.rvs random variate can take a long time depending on the values of arguments used. For example, I ran these examples in google Colab (scipy version 1.16.1) and I included zipf.rvs for comparison:
```python
%timeit scipy.stats.zipf.rvs(1.1, size=1)
> 47.3 µs ± 2.02 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10000 loops each)

%timeit scipy.stats.zipfian.rvs(1.1, n=1000, size=1)
> 1.3 ms ± 218 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1000 loops each)

%timeit scipy.stats.zipfian.rvs(1.1, n=100, size=1)
> 859 µs ± 21.7 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1000 loops each)

%timeit scipy.stats.zipfian.rvs(1.1, n=10, size=1)
> 662 µs ± 135 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1000 loops each)

%timeit scipy.stats.zipfian.rvs(0.9, n=10, size=1)
> 1.57 ms ± 219 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1000 loops each)

%timeit scipy.stats.zipfian.rvs(0.5, n=10, size=1)
> 1.63 ms ± 235 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1000 loops each)
```
So it seems like larger `n` and smaller `a` take longer to run. This makes some sense in terms of computing the generalized harmonic number: a larger `n` adds more terms to the sum of the harmonic number. I'm not entirely sure why a smaller `a` results in a longer time, but maybe it's because the sum does not converge as quickly?

### Describe the solution you'd like.

It would be useful if the zipfian random variates could be generated on a similar time scale as the zipf random variates (10's of microseconds per rvs). Right now it's not practical to generate around 1 million random variates (which I use to create toy datasets). One way to do this is to approximate the generalized harmonic number, as was done in this medium blog: https://jasoncrease.medium.com/zipf-54912d5651cc. I've been using this solution to generate zipfian random variates whenever I have `a < 1`. The author notes that this approximation is inaccurate for large `a` and small `n`.

### Describe alternatives you've considered.

For large `n` and `a > 1`, the zipfian distribution converges to the zipf/zeta distribution, but my work has needed to use `a < 1`. That's where the solution from the medium blog has been useful the most.

### Additional context (e.g. screenshots, GIFs)

_No response_

title: ENH: Replace references to AS by references to digital library of mathematical functions
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

The documentation refers regularly to the handbook by Abramowitz and Stegun (AS) which definitely is an excellent resource. However, not many users will have access to the original version of the handbook to which the SciPy documentation refers. A revised version of the content of AS is available for 15 years now as the Digital Library of Mathematical Functions (DLMF) at dlmf.nist.gov. As this resource is hosted by NIST, it can be considered to have long term support (even though given the present political situation one never can know for sure). In my opinion,  it would make a lot of sense to migrate from AS to DLMF as this would provide direct online access to the equations referred to in the documentation.

### Describe the solution you'd like.

Since the Scipy documentation contains a large number of references to AS, it would make sense to start with a consistent replacement in part of the documentation. A natural place to start would be the special functions subpackage. As far as I can tell, the chapter of AS which has undergone the biggest structural revision concerns the orthogonal polynomials. It therefore would make sense in a first step to try to replace AS references by DLMF references in the documentation of orthogonal polynomials. If no problems are encountered there, it is highly likely that the replacement will work out for at least the rest of the special functions documentation. If the replacement of the AS references is considered worthwhile, I would volunteer to work on it. 

### Describe alternatives you've considered.

The alternative is to keep the references to AS as they are. A reason to do so could be that books usually have higher long term stability than internet resources. However, I would consider DLMF to be very stable also with respect to the links to individual formulae.

### Additional context (e.g. screenshots, GIFs)

_No response_

title: BUG: stats: `trapezoid.fit()` doesn't converge
body: ### Describe your issue. `scipy.stats.trapezoid` was implemented in 2016 in issue https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/6028. However, `.fit` doesn't converge when no starting value is given for args `c` and `d`. I've only come across this issue with `scipy.stats.trapezoid`, not with any other `scipy.stats` distributions. The issue happens both when the default MLE is used and when Method of Moments is used. In both cases it can easily be fixed by specifying some args0. Following the principle that things should "just work" out of the box in the majority of cases, I propose that sensible default args0 be chosen. Then, when `fit` is called without specifying args0, those default args0 can be used for the MLE minimisation so that it will converge. Here is the output of the following code which illustrates the issue: The green line is a fit with no args0, and always results in `c=d=1` i.e. a triangular distribution. This happens for all the cases tried. The red dotted line is using a fit with args0 specified (`c=0.33, d=0.66`), and while not perfect always fits much better, as reflected in the much higher log likelihood. ### Reproducing Code Example ```python import scipy.stats import numpy as np import matplotlib.pyplot as plt cd = scipy.stats.uniform(0,1).rvs(size=(10,2), random_state=0).round(2) cd = np.sort(cd, axis=1) fig, axs = plt.subplots(len(cd), 2, figsize=(10, 2*len(cd)), sharex=True) starting_cds = [ (), # (.99,), # (.99,.99), # (.01, .01), (.33,.66), ] methods = [ "MM", # "MLE", ] x = np.linspace(-.01, 1.01, 103) for i, (c,d) in enumerate(cd): true_dist = scipy.stats.trapezoid(c,d, 0,1) rvs = true_dist.rvs(1_000, random_state=0) # print("true generating distribution", true_dist.args, -scipy.stats.trapezoid.nnlf(true_dist.args, rvs), sep=" \t") axs[i][0].hist(rvs, bins=50, density=True, alpha=.2, label='rvs') axs[i][0].plot(x, true_dist.pdf(x), label=f'true pdf. LL={-scipy.stats.trapezoid.nnlf(true_dist.args, rvs):.2f}') axs[i][1].hist(rvs, bins=50, density=True, cumulative=True, alpha=.2, label='rvs') axs[i][1].plot(x, true_dist.cdf(x), label=f'true cdf. (c,d)={c,d}. LL={-scipy.stats.trapezoid.nnlf(true_dist.args, rvs):.2f}') for starting_cd in starting_cds: for method in methods: fitted_args = scipy.stats.trapezoid.fit(rvs, *starting_cd, floc=0, scale=1, method=method) fitted_dist = scipy.stats.trapezoid(*fitted_args) # print(starting_cd, method, fitted_dist.args, -scipy.stats.trapezoid.nnlf(fitted_dist.args, rvs), sep=" \t") label = f'fitted cdf. Method={method}. Starting (c,d)={starting_cd}. LL={-scipy.stats.trapezoid.nnlf(fitted_dist.args, rvs):.2f}' axs[i][0].plot(x, fitted_dist.pdf(x), ls=":", ) axs[i][1].plot(x, fitted_dist.cdf(x), ls=":", label=label) axs[i][1].ecdf(rvs, alpha=.4, label='ecdf') # axs[i][0].legend(fontsize='xx-small') axs[i][1].legend(fontsize='x-small', loc='center left', bbox_to_anchor=(1, 0.5)) axs[0][0].set_title('pdf') axs[0][1].set_title('cdf') plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` ### Error message ```shell na ``` ### SciPy/NumPy/Python version and system information ```shell 1.13.0 1.26.4 sys.version_info(major=3, minor=12, micro=3, releaselevel='final', serial=0) ```

title: ENH: improve fitting of scipy.stats distributions using appropriate bounds and constraints
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

This follows on from a separate issue https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/23503 in which this question was touched upon. I thought I'd pull all the relevant comments into one new thread so they don't get lost in that discussion, as there are valuable points scattered over the [issue](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/23503) and additional points in the associated [pull request](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/23504).

The conversation there was about trapezoid fit failing to converge. @nickodell pointed out that the solver's simplex included out-of-bounds values (bounds: `c,d ∈ [0,1]`):
> > Looking at the behavior of the Nelder-Mead solver, it looks like c = 1 d = 1 causes the initial simplex to be the following:
> >
> > ```
> > initial_sim [[1.   1.  ]
> >  [1.05 1.  ]
> >  [1.   1.05]]
> > ```
> @nickodell do you happen to know whether the solver is bounded? I'd've assumed that when minimising negative log likelihood, that it would give the solver bounds for the args. These bounds could be simply taken for every distribution from its `_shape_info`. But from that simplex that doesn't seem to be done?
> 
> (In fact I'd consider giving the solver constraints as well as bounds. For example for trapezoid we must have `c<=d`, which isn't expressed by the bounds (`c,d ∈ [0,1]`) alone. These constraints can be taken using each distribution's `_argcheck` as a constraint function, i.e. constrain the search to `c,d` values for which `_argcheck` returns `True`. However using constraints means limits on which solver algorithms can be used). 

 _Originally posted by @Gabriel-Kissin in [#23503](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/23503#issuecomment-3221501030)_

> I can tell you that the Nelder-Mead optimizer contains code to avoid this case when you provide x0 which is right up against the bounds. (See #20345) However, fit() doesn't appear to supply any bounds to Nelder-Mead, so that code never runs. I couldn't tell you why - I mostly follow scipy.optimize development. Someone with scipy.stats knowledge may be able to tell you.
> 
> Using constraints would pretty significantly reduce your options on which optimizers you could use. Only COBYLA, COBYQA, SLSQP and trust-constr support them.
> 
> Of those options, I would probably not pick COBYLA or SLSQP, as they have a number of very weird numerical behaviors. (e.g in some cases they can minimize the function `f(x)`, but not the function `1000000*f(x)`.) That leaves trust-constr and COBYQA, which I suspect weren't chosen due to the fact that they're quite slow. 

 _Originally posted by @nickodell in [#23503](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/23503#issuecomment-3221529872)_


> > Using constraints would pretty significantly reduce your options on which optimizers you could use. Only COBYLA, COBYQA, SLSQP and trust-constr support them.
> 
> Or `differential_evolution`, which is the slowest of them all. I think `trust-constr` is probably the best to use if there aren't really any local minima, `differential_evolution` is much more likely to find the global minimum.
> 
> `nelder-mead` simply clips values to the bounds. It doesn't [look like](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/main/scipy/stats/_distn_infrastructure.py#L2770) bounds are supplied to the optimizer.
> 
> In that vein, adding in constraints/bounds/etc looks to be a bit of work. 

 _Originally posted by @andyfaff in [#23503](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/23503#issuecomment-3222057608)_



> For trapezoid, setting c = 1 and d = 1 causes at least one value in the initial simplex to be outside the bounds of valid parameters.
> 
> It looks like a similar problem can be found in the default settings for the following other distributions:
> 
> * triang
> * ksone
> * kstwo
> 
> See the following program for how I found this list: https://gist.github.com/nickodell/70f1172e3c667e8d34008933f303870c 

 _Originally posted by @nickodell in [#23503](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/23503#issuecomment-3222194111)_


> @mdhaber, does it make sense to start adding more complexity to the fitting mechanism here? There are several situations where using the following could make sense:
> 
> - one shape parameter should be less than the other (`LinearConstraint`/`NonlinearConstraint`)
> - where shape parameters should remain within limits (`Bounds`)
> 
> Or would effort be better directed towards the new `ContinuousDistribution` infrastructure?

_Originally posted by @andyfaff in https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/23504#issuecomment-3226712872_
            

### Describe the solution you'd like.

- Bounds: 
  - the values for the bounds are already known for each distribution, as mentioned, in `_shape_info` 
  - parsing bounds from `_shape_info` and passing them to the solver should be relatively simple.
  - bounds may be helpful not just for shape params but also for `scale>0`.
  - Reducing the search space should accelerate convergence and improve robustness.
- Constraints:
  - E.g. for the trapezoid distribution, we must have c<=d, which isn't expressed by the bounds `c,d ∈ [0,1]` alone
  - This could be given to the solver like `constraints = [{"type": "ineq", "fun": lambda params: params[1] - params[0]}]  # d - c >= 0`
  - To avoid specifying such constraints for each distribution, we could perhaps use the `_argcheck` method each distribution already has (would it matter that `_argcheck` only returns bool True/False instead of a float indicating how far from the constraint we are / differentiable function?)
  - This could be given to the solver like `constraints = [{"type": "ineq", "fun": lambda params: distribution._argcheck(*params)}]  # general for any dist`


I imagine implementing bounds would be easier than implementing constraints. It would also place less limitations on which solvers can be used as discussed. Giving at least bounds does seem like an obvious thing to do

If just bounds were implemented, would nelder-mead still work? Given that its way of doing bounds is simply to clip the simplex. In the trapezoid case, such clipping would result in a simplex like
```
[[1.   1.  ]    # [[1.   1.  ]
 [1.   1.  ]    #  [1.05 1.  ]
 [1.   1.  ]]   #  [1.   1.05]]
```
which wouldn't help the solver much... so perhaps a solver which has proper support for bounds would be  necessary

title: DOC: SLSQP - different sign convention of Lagrange multiplier
body:
### Describe your issue.

The function `minimize(f, x0, constraints=cons, bounds=bounds, method="SLSQP") ` returns correct optimal point, but Lagrangian multiplier, which corresponds to equality constraint, has incorrect sign (negative value expected). 

The task was taken from https://coin-or.github.io/pulp/CaseStudies/a_blending_problem.html#simplified-formulation.

Lagrange multiplier value must be -0.01633332.

Solution with sympy and scipy in the attached file. I also double checked my solution with ChatGPT.

[lagr_multiplier_sign.pdf](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/22005766/lagr_multiplier_sign.pdf)

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
from scipy.optimize import Bounds, minimize
import numpy as np

f = lambda x: 0.013*x[0] + 0.008*x[1]

cons = [
    {
        "type": "ineq", "fun": lambda x: 0.1*x[0] + 0.2*x[1] - 8
    },
    {
        "type": "ineq", "fun": lambda x: 0.08*x[0] + 0.1*x[1] - 6
    },
    {
        "type": "ineq", "fun": lambda x: -0.001*x[0] - 0.005*x[1] + 2
    },
    {
        "type": "ineq", "fun": lambda x: -0.002*x[0] - 0.005*x[1] + 0.4
    },
    {
        "type": "eq", "fun": lambda x: x[0] + x[1] - 100
    }
]

bounds = [[0, None], [0, None]]
x0 = np.array([10, 10])
result = minimize(f, x0, constraints=cons, bounds=bounds, method="SLSQP")

"""
message: Optimization terminated successfully
     success: True
      status: 0
         fun: 0.9666666509031268
           x: [ 3.333e+01  6.667e+01]
         nit: 9
         jac: [ 1.300e-02  8.000e-03]
        nfev: 27
        njev: 9
 multipliers: [ 1.633e-02  0.000e+00  0.000e+00  0.000e+00  1.667e+00]
"""
```

### SciPy/NumPy/Python version and system information

```shell
1.16.1 2.3.2 sys.version_info(major=3, minor=12, micro=3, releaselevel='final', serial=0)
```

title: SHGO: issue passing args to objective function
body:
### Describe your issue.

SHGO seems to have a problem when passing a (function, jac, hess) with args. In the example below, `args = (a)`, where `a` is a float.

During global minimization, the class SHGO creates the attribute:

`self.func = _FunctionWrapper(func, args)`

which works well.

However, during local minimization (SHGO.minimization), the method `scipy.optimize.minimize` is called as:

`lres = minimize(self.func, x_min, **self.minimizer_kwargs)`

The method `minimize` wraps self.func as:

`self._wrapped_fun = _ScalarFunctionWrapper(fun, args)`

So, the original function goes trough the process:

f = original_function --> f1 = _FunctionWrapper(f, args) --> f2 = _ScalarFunctionWrapper(f1, args)

Then, when the line in the *error message* below `fx = self.f(np.copy(x), *self.args)` is executed, self = f2, and self.f = f1, and `f1.__call__(self, x)` only receive 2 args {self, x}, but 3 were given {self, np.copy(x), a}; recall that args = (a).

-----------

if `args` is not passed to `minimizer_kwargs`, then it fails because 'jac' misses 1 required positional argument 'a'.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
import scipy

def f(x, a):
    return np.sum((x - a)**2)

def jac(x, a):
    return 2*(x - a)

def hessp(x, p, a):
    return 2*p

a = 1.1
res = scipy.optimize.shgo(
    f,
    bounds=[(-5, 5)],
    args=(a,),
    minimizer_kwargs={
        'method': 'Newton-CG',
        'args': (a,),
        'jac': jac,
        'hessp': hessp
    })
```
