```shell
  File "/users/rsg/peland/miniconda3/envs/chl/lib/python3.14/site-packages/scipy/interpolate/_fitpack_repro.py", line 1158, in make_splrep
    x, y, w, k, s, xb, xe = _validate_inputs(x, y, w, k, s, xb, xe,
                            ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
                                             parametric=False, periodic=periodic)
                                             ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/users/rsg/peland/miniconda3/envs/chl/lib/python3.14/site-packages/scipy/interpolate/_fitpack_repro.py", line 155, in _validate_inputs
    raise ValueError("First and last points does not match which is required "
                     "for `bc_type='periodic'`.")
ValueError: First and last points does not match which is required for `bc_type='periodic'`.
```

title: ENH: add more extrapolate options to Bspline (and probably others)
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

Extrapolating a high-order polynomial (e.g. cubic) is generally a bad idea, more robust is to extrapolate at a lower order.

### Describe the solution you'd like.

I propose an extrapolate_order parameter. 0 would be constant, 1 linear etc. None would give the only current option of using the full polynomial order.

### Describe alternatives you've considered.

Doing linear extrapolation myself using spl.derivative.

### Additional context (e.g. screenshots, GIFs)

_No response_

title: BUG: Non-singular matrix incorrectly flagged as singular in _multivariate.py
body:
### Describe your issue.

In computing the logpdf for the below values I am getting a singularity flagged with scipy when the diagonal matrix is otherwise invertible through numpy.

Going into the _multivariate.py script and changing cond from the calculated 2.22e-10 to double precision machine epsilon 2.22e-16 fixes this.

I notice also that cond and rcond cannot be passed through from the logpdf method so I cannot directly overwrite this.  What are your thoughts on passing **kwds through on methods _process_parameters, _PSD, logpdf, and pdf so a user can override cond/rcond?

There are two potential answers here, change the value of cond that gets calculated or allow the user to override cond/rcond.  If the latter I can submit a PR no problem.

Cheers!

title: ENH: optimize.root: add exact Newton method
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

Inside `scipy.optimize.root` are a lot of different solvers for inexact newton algorithms like broyden1, broyden2, anderson, linearmixing, diagbroyden, excitingmixing, krylov, but there is no option for the actual/exact newton method.
As there is already an option to provide the jacobi matrix, it should be an option to use the exact newton method, without approximating the jacobi matrix via an inexact newton method.

I am not fully sure if that is a feature request (i.e. there is actually no way to call an exact newton method) or if this is a documentation problem (i.e. this option exist, but is not documented).

title: DOC: optimize.root: inexact newton methods and the `jac` option
body:
### Issue with current documentation:

Inside the documentation of `scipy.optimize.root` it is not really clear which methods actually use the user provided jacobi matrix, and if they use it, what they do with it. 
Looking in the code, it is clear, that the derivative free solver `df-sane` and the inexact newton methods do not use the jacobian as `_warn_jac_unused(jac, method)` is called. Further, it is not clear, how the other methods use the jacobian, or what they do, if the user does not provide it.

I think a little more information would help here, e.g. add some text to the documentation of the `jac` option reading something like:
only used for the solvers <list of solvers>. If any of these solvers that uses the jacobian is selected without providing the jacobian, then <what happens here? probably approximated?>

### Idea or request for content:

_No response_

title: DOC: optimize: The convergence table for root finders is hard to understand
body: ### Issue with current documentation: The [API docs for the root finders](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/optimize.html) say the following: >The table below lists situations and appropriate methods, along with asymptotic convergence rates per iteration (and per function evaluation) for successful convergence to a simple root(*). Bisection is the slowest of them all, adding one bit of accuracy for each function evaluation, but is guaranteed to converge. The other bracketing methods all (eventually) increase the number of accurate bits by about 50% for every function evaluation. The derivative-based methods, all built on newton, can converge quite quickly if the initial value is close to the root. They can also be applied to functions defined on (a subset of) the complex plane. > > I find this confusing for a few reasons: * I expected the footnote (*) to refer to text below the table, rather than text above. * I did not understand that the text "along with asymptotic convergence rates per iteration (and per function evaluation)" means that the section in parens is refering to the part of the table in parens, and the previous part outside of parens is. I only figured this out by reading the associated PR, #8876. ### Idea or request for content: * I would suggest removing the convergence rate per iteration to simplify the table. This is not something that one can compare, apples-to-apples, across solvers, as some have more complex loops which evaluate the user's function multiple times. Convergence per function evaluation would be a more meaningful metric. * I would suggest adding a definition of what kind of convergence is being measured. * I would suggest moving the footnote below the table. ### Additional context (e.g. screenshots, GIFs) _No response_

title: BUG: stats.kstest: issues with `method` argument
body:
### Describe your issue.

There are several issues with the `method` argument of `scipy.stats.kstest`[^1]:

- [x] (1) `kstest`/`ks_1samp` treat all strings other than `asymp`, `auto`, and `exact` as `approx`; there is no input validation and `approx` is treated as an `else` case.
- [ ] (2) `kstest`/`ks_1samp` ignores the `method` argument when `alternative` is not two-sided.
- [ ] (3) `kstest`/`ks_1samp` apparently has no need of a `method` argument because the exact distribution of the statistic can be calculated efficiently - faster than either approximation in limited testing.
- [ ] (4) `kstest` documentation suggests that `method='approx'` is accepted unconditionally, but it is rejected when performing a two-sample test.

It's also worth noting that the naming `method='asymp'` does not follow the same convention used by `kendalltau`, `wilcoxon`, `mannwhitneyu`, `method='asymptotic'`.

This may not be exhaustive; I just noticed these during gh-24726.

[^1]: setting aside the issues with the KS test itself and having a combined `kstest` "convenience" function

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats
rng = np.random.default_rng(594235924652)
x = rng.random(10)
y = rng.random(10)

# 1. arbitrary strings are treated as `method='approx'`
np.testing.assert_equal(
    stats.kstest(x, stats.norm.cdf, method='any_other_string'),
    stats.kstest(x, stats.norm.cdf, method='approx'),
)  # passes

# 2. `method` is ignored when `alternative` is specified
np.testing.assert_equal(
    stats.kstest(x, stats.norm.cdf, method='approx', alternative='greater'),
    stats.kstest(x, stats.norm.cdf, method='exact', alternative='greater')
)  # passes

# 3. `method` is of questionable utility
%timeit stats.kstest(rng.random(1000000), stats.norm.cdf, method='exact')
# 113 ms ± 962 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10 loops each)

%timeit stats.kstest(rng.random(1000000), stats.norm.cdf, method='asymp')
# 125 ms ± 17.4 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10 loops each)

# 4. `method='approx'` is not accepted for two-sample test
stats.kstest(x, y, method='approx') 
# ValueError: Invalid value for mode: approx
```

### Error message

```shell
-
```

title: ENH: interpolate: CuPy delegation
body:
There are classes  in `scipy.interpolate`s public API that are available in CuPy for which delegation is not yet set up. Checking the [comparison table](https://docs.cupy.dev/en/latest/reference/comparison.html#interpolation) in the CuPy docs, I see the following things which do not currently have CuPy support in SciPy:

* [Akima1DInterpolator](https://docs.cupy.dev/en/latest/reference/generated/cupyx.scipy.interpolate.Akima1DInterpolator.html#cupyx.scipy.interpolate.Akima1DInterpolator)
* [BPoly](https://docs.cupy.dev/en/latest/reference/generated/cupyx.scipy.interpolate.BPoly.html#cupyx.scipy.interpolate.BPoly)
* [BSpline](https://docs.cupy.dev/en/latest/reference/generated/cupyx.scipy.interpolate.BSpline.html#cupyx.scipy.interpolate.BSpline)
* [BarycentricInterpolator](https://docs.cupy.dev/en/latest/reference/generated/cupyx.scipy.interpolate.BarycentricInterpolator.html#cupyx.scipy.interpolate.BarycentricInterpolator)
* [CloughTocher2DInterpolator](https://docs.cupy.dev/en/latest/reference/generated/cupyx.scipy.interpolate.CloughTocher2DInterpolator.html#cupyx.scipy.interpolate.CloughTocher2DInterpolator)
* [CubicHermiteSpline](https://docs.cupy.dev/en/latest/reference/generated/cupyx.scipy.interpolate.CubicHermiteSpline.html#cupyx.scipy.interpolate.CubicHermiteSpline)
* [CubicSpline](https://docs.cupy.dev/en/latest/reference/generated/cupyx.scipy.interpolate.CubicSpline.html#cupyx.scipy.interpolate.CubicSpline)
* [KroghInterpolator](https://docs.cupy.dev/en/latest/reference/generated/cupyx.scipy.interpolate.KroghInterpolator.html#cupyx.scipy.interpolate.KroghInterpolator)
* [LinearNDInterpolator](https://docs.cupy.dev/en/latest/reference/generated/cupyx.scipy.interpolate.LinearNDInterpolator.html#cupyx.scipy.interpolate.LinearNDInterpolator)
* [NdBSpline](https://docs.cupy.dev/en/latest/reference/generated/cupyx.scipy.interpolate.NdBSpline.html#cupyx.scipy.interpolate.NdBSpline)
* [NdPPoly](https://docs.cupy.dev/en/latest/reference/generated/cupyx.scipy.interpolate.NdPPoly.html#cupyx.scipy.interpolate.NdPPoly)
* [NearestNDInterpolator](https://docs.cupy.dev/en/latest/reference/generated/cupyx.scipy.interpolate.NearestNDInterpolator.html#cupyx.scipy.interpolate.NearestNDInterpolator)
* [PPoly](https://docs.cupy.dev/en/latest/reference/generated/cupyx.scipy.interpolate.PPoly.html#cupyx.scipy.interpolate.PPoly)
* [PchipInterpolator](https://docs.cupy.dev/en/latest/reference/generated/cupyx.scipy.interpolate.PchipInterpolator.html#cupyx.scipy.interpolate.PchipInterpolator)
* [RegularGridInterpolator](https://docs.cupy.dev/en/latest/reference/generated/cupyx.scipy.interpolate.RegularGridInterpolator.html#cupyx.scipy.interpolate.RegularGridInterpolator)

The following classes are also in CuPy, but I did not include them in the above list because they are designated as legacy and therefore are considered out of scope for array API support: `InterpolatedUnivariateSpline`, `UnivariateSpline`.

The following functions in `scipy.interpolate` are either available in CuPy, or could be implemented in terms of the above classes which are in CuPy: `approximate_taylor_polynomial`, `barycentric_interpolate`, `griddata`, `interpn`, `krogh_interpolate`, `make_interp_spline`, `pchip_interpolate`.

`RbfInterpolator` is in CuPy but was made array-agnostic through some nice work by @ev-br in https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/23746. I think some of the above interpolators could also be made array-agnostic, or mostly array-agnostic with delegation for some linear algebra primitives not in the standard, which would be a worthy addition. This issue concerns CuPy delegation though. I hope to eventually to submit issues for individual interpolator classes that I believe could be made array-agnostic.

@ogrisel pointed out that CuPy support in SciPy for `BSpline` in particular would be helpful for Scikit-Learn, so I plan to submit a small PR first only for `BSpline`. This should be much easier to review than a large PR for everything, and reviews of subsequent PRs could check against the template established in the initial `BSpline` PR.

title: ENH: Include special functions for Clebsch-Gordon coefficients, also Wigner 3j/6j/9j symbols
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

There is no canonical, numerically oriented, python package for calculating these terms that have broad application.  

### Describe the solution you'd like.

Functions in `scipy.special` for calculating Clebsch-Gordon coefficients and Wigner 3j/6j/9j symbols with well-referenced citations explaining the numerical implementation.

### Describe alternatives you've considered.

The best place to go is `sympy.physics.wigner` which has these functions. But the computations there are symbolic, which is not necessarily what you always want. Plus it feels like a waste to install the whole `sympy` package with all of its symbolic machinery just for these specific functions used in a non-symbolic way. A numerical approach would be better. `py3nj` and `wigners` packages exist. `py3nj` doesn't have pre-built wheels for Windows. `wigners` doesn't have support for half-integer angular momenta. These are also small packages that I can't tell if they're really well-maintained. 

The `wigners` package pypi page cites a paper it uses for its numerical implementation and gives suggestions for how the implementation in `wigners` could be made more general. `py3nj` cites wrapping "the original Fortran implementation in slatec". These give hints about how `scipy` could implement these functions.


### Additional context (e.g. screenshots, GIFs)

_No response_

title: MAINT/DOC: optimize.root: sparse arrays as `jac` unsupported with `hybr`
body:
### Describe your issue.

Using the 'scipy.optimize.root' function with the default `'hybr'` method and providing the jacobi matrix in as `scipy.sparse.sparray` format, throws and unexpected shape miss-match error.

(in addition, the error message is wrong, stating `'fsolve'` instead of `'root'` and `'fprime'` instead of `'jac'`)

The problem lies inside the function `_check_func` in the file `scipy.optimize._minpack_py.py`. There the line
```python
res = atleast_1d(thefunc(*((x0[:numinputs],) + args)))
```
causes the problem, as `atleast_1d` converts the sparse jacobi matrix of correct shape into a numpy array of shape `(1,)` with its sole element being the jacobi matrix.

I did not try other matrix formats, such as `scipy.sparse.spmatrix`, but i suspect they wont work as well.
This problem does not seam to be with all methods, at least `'broyden1'` works, but i did not check other methods besides those two.

EDIT: Of course `'broyden1'` works, as it does not use the `jac` argument.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
from scipy.optimize import root
from scipy.sparse import eye_array

fun = lambda x: x
jac = lambda x: eye_array(x.shape[0])
root(fun=fun, x0=np.ones(3), jac=jac, method='hybr')
```

### Error message

```shell
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/home/moritz/miniconda3/envs/mypdes/lib/python3.12/site-packages/scipy/optimize/_minpack_py.py", line 38, in _check_func
    raise TypeError(msg)
TypeError: fsolve: there is a mismatch between the input and output shape of the 'fprime' argument '<lambda>'.Shape should be (3, 3) but it is (1,).
```

### SciPy/NumPy/Python version and system information

```shell
1.17
```

title: BUG: optimize.root: minpack solvers Jacobian count is off by one
body:
### Describe your issue.

It appears that the njev value in the solution object is one too low for methods hybr and lm.

In the following code example, hybr evaluates jac() 2 times, but reports an njev of 1. See line in output, `njev int 1 ext 2`.

In the following code example, lm evaluates jac() 7 times, but reports an njev of 6. See line in output, `njev int 6 ext 7`.

The issue appears to be a call from `_check_func()` which is not counted in the total.

For the cases I have examined, nfev appears to be correct for all solvers available in `scipy.optimize.root()`. njev is not used by other solvers, so this problem is confined to just lm and hybr.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
from scipy import optimize

# Note: copied from first example here
# https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.optimize.root.html
def fun(x):
#    print("called fun")
    return [x[0]  + 0.5 * (x[0] - x[1])**3 - 1.0,
            0.5 * (x[1] - x[0])**3 + x[1]]

def jac(x):
    print("called jacobian", x)
    return np.array([[1 + 1.5 * (x[0] - x[1])**2,
                      -1.5 * (x[0] - x[1])**2],
                     [-1.5 * (x[1] - x[0])**2,
                      1 + 1.5 * (x[1] - x[0])**2]])


def count_calls(func, name):
    def inner(*args, **kwargs):
        nonlocal inner
        inner.count += 1
        return func(*args, **kwargs)
    inner.count = 0
    return inner

# All methods
# methods = ['hybr', 'lm', 'broyden1', 'broyden2', 'anderson', 'linearmixing', 'diagbroyden', 'excitingmixing', 'krylov', 'df-sane']
# Methods that make use of jacobian
methods = ['hybr', 'lm']
for method in methods:
    print(f"{method=}")
    fun_count = count_calls(fun, 'fun')
    jac_count = count_calls(jac, 'jacobian')
    sol = optimize.root(fun_count, [0, 0], jac=jac_count, method=method)
    nfev_internal = sol.nfev
    # If solution does not include njev, assume it is 0
    njev_internal = getattr(sol, 'njev', 0)
    nfev_external = fun_count.count
    njev_external = jac_count.count
    print(f"nfev int {nfev_internal} ext {nfev_external}")
    print(f"njev int {njev_internal} ext {njev_external}")
    print("result")
    print(sol)
    print()
```

```pycon
>>> from scipy.spatial.transform import Rotation as R
>>> p = R.from_quat([0, 0, 0, 1])
>>> p.approx_equal(p)
True
```


1.17.1:

```pycon
>>> p = R.from_quat([0, 0, 0, 1])
>>> p.approx_equal(p)
array([ True])
```

title: BUG: `special.lambertw` gives `nan` for -1/e
body:
Using the latest scipy version 1.17.1, I get:
```
>>> from math import e
... from scipy.special import lambertw
... lambertw(-1.0/e)
... 
np.complex128(nan+nanj)
```
From the definition of the Lambert W function, one would expect the value -1.0.
Either adding or substracting `1e-12` to the argument -1/e, will return a value very close to -1.0.

Testing the same function in mpmath, i.e. `from mpmath import lambertw`, I don't see this error.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
from math import e
from scipy.special import lambertw
print(lambertw(-1.0/e))
```

### Error message

```shell
(nan+nanj) # not an error, but the wrong answer
```

title: BUG: Unstable behavior of `stats.mannwhitneyu` from v1.17.0
body:
### Describe your issue.

Hello,

From v1.17.0, `scipy.stats.mannwhitney` can be unstable and return (measurable) different results whether the input is `float32` or `float64`, even if composed of round numbers only.

I believe this is due to [this line](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/8c75ae75176236f233824e9a0483c26a69e6dfec/scipy/stats/_mannwhitneyu.py#L449) where the ranks are cast to the input's dtype, which was introduced in v1.17.0 through [23870](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/23870/changes#top). If input is `float32` and contains enough elements, ranksums (hence u-statistic) differ. 
Because not an overflow (but more a floating point rounding error) no warning is issued (which is the case if input is `float16`. Overflow warning is issued at `U2 = n1 * n2 - U1`).

MRE below (note that p-values do differ, consequently).

`_rankdata` returns ranks as `float64`. Removing this `xp.astype` would restore a stable behavior, but I don't know the rationale behind casting ranks to the input's dtype.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
from scipy.stats import mannwhitneyu
import scipy
print(f"Scipy version: {scipy.__version__}") # => Scipy version: 1.17.0

rng = np.random.RandomState(0)
x = rng.poisson(1, size=(6000, ))  # Those are integers
y = rng.poisson(1, size=(2000, ))  # Those are integers 
np.testing.assert_allclose(x.astype(np.float32), x.astype(np.float64), atol=0, rtol=0)  # passes, because round numbers
np.testing.assert_allclose(y.astype(np.float32), y.astype(np.float64), atol=0, rtol=0)  # passes, because round numbers

# Run MWU test
res_f32 = mannwhitneyu(x.astype(np.float32), y.astype(np.float32), alternative="two-sided", method="asymptotic", use_continuity=True)
res_f64 = mannwhitneyu(x.astype(np.float64), y.astype(np.float64), alternative="two-sided", method="asymptotic", use_continuity=True)
print(f"{res_f32.statistic}") # => 6091892.0
print(f"{res_f64.statistic}") # => 6091893.5
```

### Error message

```shell
No error message.
```

### SciPy/NumPy/Python version and system information

title: ENH:  Add Inverse Chirp Z-Transform (ICZT) to scipy.signal
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

scipy.signal provides CZT and czt() for the forward Chirp Z-Transform, but there is no inverse counterpart (ICZT / iczt). This makes round-trip frequency↔time domain processing impossible within scipy alone.

### Describe the solution you'd like.

Add an ICZT class and iczt() function to scipy.signal.

### Describe alternatives you've considered.

Some packages are available on github. But they don't seem to have solved the periodicity problem, so it gives the same result as FFT.

### Additional context (e.g. screenshots, GIFs)

What I'm really curious about is whether your reason for not including this is related to a problem in the equation or the result it gives?

title: BUG: `scipy.sparse.linalg.tfqmr` raises `UnboundLocalError` when `maxiter=0` and `show=True`
body:
### Describe your issue.

When executing the `tfqmr` solver with `maxiter=0`, a `UnboundLocalError` raises because the `iter` variable is not previously initialized. 

The error is because `maxiter=0`, `iter` is not initialized in `for iter in range(maxiter):` [line 136](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/main/scipy/sparse/linalg/_isolve/tfqmr.py#L136). Later, the parameter `show=True` executes the following block at the end of the `tfqmr` function:

```
if (show):
    print("TFQMR: Linear solve not converged due to reach MAXIT "
            f"iterations {iter+1}")
```

Similar to #23748, which is about the same exception being raised when passing `maxiter=0` to another API. 

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
from scipy.sparse.linalg import tfqmr

def test_tfqmr_zero_iter_show_true_basic():
    A = np.eye(3)
    b = np.ones(3)
    try:
        x, info = tfqmr(A, b, maxiter=0, show=True)
    except Exception as e:
        # Convert any runtime exception into a test failure (AssertionError)
        raise AssertionError(f"tfqmr raised an unexpected exception: {type(e).__name__}: {e}")
    assert isinstance(x, np.ndarray)
    assert x.shape == b.shape
    assert isinstance(info, int)

# Execute the test when this file is run
test_tfqmr_zero_iter_show_true_basic()
```

### Error message

```shell
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
UnboundLocalError                         Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[56], line 8
      7 try:
----> 8     x, info = tfqmr(A, b, maxiter=0, show=True)
      9 except Exception as e:
     10     # Convert any runtime exception into a test failure (AssertionError)

File ~/Documents/pyExplore/py-git/lib/python3.12/site-packages/scipy/sparse/linalg/_isolve/tfqmr.py:178, in tfqmr(A, b, x0, rtol, atol, maxiter, M, callback, show)
    176 if (show):
    177     print("TFQMR: Linear solve not converged due to reach MAXIT "
--> 178           f"iterations {iter+1}")
    179 return (x, maxiter)

UnboundLocalError: cannot access local variable 'iter' where it is not associated with a value
```

### SciPy/NumPy/Python version and system information

```shell
`1.18.0.dev0+git20260309.d30677e 2.4.3 sys.version_info(major=3, minor=12, micro=3, releaselevel='final', serial=0)`
```

### Tool-Use Disclosure
This bug was identified by an automated testing tool currently being developed by our research team. 
Before reporting it, we reviewed the code and checked the latest development version of SciPy to confirm the issue.

title: Tracking spmatrix deprecation progress and action items
body:
SciPy coordinating issue for deprecation of spmatrix in favor of sparray

The project to move from the sparse *matrix* API to the sparse *array* API
is ready for its next step. The sparray feature set has been
complete and stable since v1.15. Most features were implemented
well before that, as early as v1.8. The migration guide has
been available since v1.15. We have helped a number of libraries
make the conversion to using sparray, including
networkx, sksparse, dipy, scikit-image, cvxpy, pyamg, many pysal libraries, and scikit-learn.

This Issue is a first step of the deprecation process.
Feedback is welcome.  This document itself could take many forms. Do we want lists of functions to change? modules? subpackages? I intend to edit this original post as progress is made -- so let me know what you'd like to see updated/reorganized, etc in this OP.

Plan for a general order of changes:  (sections with details below)
    1) docstrings warnings and deemphasizing spmatrix
    2) deprecation warnings and adapt tests to filter warnings
    3) removal

**============= Decisions Needed ===========**
Decisions need to be made about the apporach to be taken for the following deprecations
that occur before the main deprecation.
1) construct.py functions basing output type on input type:
    - block, vstack, hstack, kron, triu, tril
    - Currently: if any input is sparse array, output is sparray. 
      If all are ndarrays, or mixed ndarray and spmatrix, output is spmatrix.
      So, default (if you want to call it that) is spmatrix.
    - [x] **Proposal:** create deprecated default warnings for all-ndarray case in 1.18, change in 1.20. 
       #24886
    - **Alternative:** change to sparray for all-ndarray case when spmatrix is removed.
2) IVP/BVP: The internally used (and helper) functions for `jacobian` use spmatrix for sparse.
   - [x] #24656 internal use. No deprecation needed. switch usage in v1.18.
3) optimize/_numdiff.py: Sparse index arrays can be int64 or int32 **unless Pythran is not installed**. 
   - [x] #24656 checks if Pythran is installed and if not, it safely converts to int32 (or raises if not safe).
4) stats/crosstab.py: currently returns spmatrix when sparse. Given it's use patterns we can switch
  to sparray without deprecation.
    - [x] #24652
5) io: `spmatrix=` kwargs in loadmat, hb_read, mmread (both matlab.mio and fast_matrix_market.mmread)
   - [x] #24644 PR decision: v1.18 deprecate default values of kwarg
   - [ ] change default in v1.20
6) KDTree: kwarg `output_type` has values "dok_matrix" or "csr_matrix".
   - [x] #24642  add support for "dok_array", "csr_array" in v.18, 
   - [ ] deprecate default value in 1.19,
   - [ ] deprecation warnings for "dok_matrix" and "csr_matrix" in v1.19,
   - [ ] change default value and remove spmatrix v1.21.

**============= Details =============**
**Documentation warnings:**
- [x] Update docs in each format class for spmatrix
- [x] Update docs in each construct.py function that selects output spmatrix based on input type.
- [x] reference/sparse.rst and create reference/spmatrix_api.rst
- Add docs warnings to functions in other parts of SciPy that use spmatrix.
    - [x] io
    - [x] sparse/linalg
    - [x] sparse/csgraph
    - [x] spatial/kdtree

**Deprecation Warnings:**
- spmatrix itself
- `construct.py` functions that produce spmatrix (spdiags, diags, eye, identity, rand, random, ...)
- Default change warnings for `_construct.py` functions that return based on input type. 
- tools of secondary and tertiary nature (mostly outside of scipy/sparse) that use sparse
- kwargs in functions that control spmatrix or sparray output
    - [ ] io
        - [x] Default for `spmatrix` kwarg. Many functions use a keyword arg that
          default to sparse matrix output. We need to deprecate the default value
          to sparse array output. Done in #24644
            - loadmat, hb_read, mmio (fast_matrix_market) mmread, mio (matlab) mmread.
        - [ ] v1.20 Change default value for spmatrix kwarg.
        - [ ] v1.19 Add deprecation warning for spmatrix kwarg taking on value "spmatrix".
        - [ ] v1.21 Remove ability to have value "spmatrix".
    - [ ] stats
        - [x] #24652 crosstab has been created with sparse matrix usage internally.
          Can likely be switched over to sparse array directly without deprecation.
        - [ ] _stats_py.py #24886 (use of legacy construct function sparse.eye that is converted to sparray 2 lines later).  No change in stats api needed.
    - [x] integrate (ivp/bvp)
        - [x] #24656 IVP and BVP use sparse matrix internally as part of the `jacobian`
          function helpers. The functions are designed to be used internally.
          Should be able to switch to sparray without deprecation -- need integrate owner to verify.
    - [x] linalg
        - [x] _eigen/arpack/arpack.py single `sparse.eye` needs to be changed to eye_array. No impact on output.
        - [x] tests
            -  tests/test_decomp.py  (use of legacy construct functions)
            -  tests/test_lapack.py  (use of legacy construct functions)
            -  tests/test_sketches.py  (use of legacy construct functions)
            -  tests/test_funm_multiply_krylov.py  (use of legacy construct functions)
            -  tests/test_norm.py  (use of legacy construct functions)
            -  tests/test_onenormest.py  (use of legacy construct functions)
            -  tests/test_special_sparse_arrays.py  (use of legacy construct functions)
            -  _dsolve/tests/test_linsolve.py  (use of legacy construct functions)
    - [ ] spatial KDTree 
        - [x] #24642 KDTree kwarg `output_type` adds sparse_array as a supported value
        - [ ] v1.19 Deprecate the kwarg values `output_type = "*_matrix"` for * in ("dok", "csr")
            and deprecate the default value of kwarg `output_type` "dok_matrix" -> "dok_array"
        - [ ] v1.21 Remove sparse matrix output_type values and switch default
    -  ~~optimize~~  Orthogonal decision to sparray changes!
        - ~~Need to check for Pythran. If not there, must check that 
            sparse input has int32 index arrays, or safely cast to int32.~~

**Test filters for warnings:**
- List of tests that fail due to deprecation (checkbox means the filters appear in PR #24888) :
    - [x] scipy/sparse/tests/ Many test classes in test_base.py. test_array_api.py, test_csr.py
        - test_base.py & test_64bit.py: Matrix test classes
        - test_array_api.py: tests that illustrate spmatrix is not array_api.
        - test_construct.py: 
        - test_csr.py & test_csc & test_dok.py. One test each.
        - test_extract.py, test_minmax1d.py:  one test each. test_matrix_io.py: 4 tests.
    - [x] sparse/linalg:
        - tests/test_sparse_norm
    - [x] sparse/csgraph:  (all are testing both spmatrix and sparray output types)
        - test_asymmetric_laplacian
        - test_symmetric_laplacian
        - test_format
        - test_return_type
    - [x] optimize/tests/test_optimize
        - test_sparse_hessian (tests both types)
    - [x] spatial/tests/test_kdtree.py
        - test_ckdtree_return_types

**Timing**
The timing of the deprecation/removal cycle (as aimed for by the sparse array group) could be:
- Documentation v1.18 (descriptions of spmatrix moved to separate pages, doc warnings of upcoming deprecation)
- DeprecationWarnings added in v1.19, with appropriate filtering of warnings in tests
- The warnings will say: Removal will not occur earlier than v1.21.

Removal in v1.21 with normal cadence puts the first release without spmatrix at Jan 2028. This may change based on user/maintainer reaction.

title: BENCH: switch timing from spmatrix to sparray
body:
The Benchmarks overwhelmingly use spmatrix for the sparse algorithm timing needs.
We should be shifting that to sparray. The results will be mostly be faster, but not by very much.
We have already used the benchmarking suite to compare sparray and spmatrix locally. So the change in the library benchmarks should be aimed at allowing comparisons over time & commits rather than between the interfaces.  We can and will continue timing between the interfaces -- but that's not the goal of the benchmark suite itself.

How should we handle the switchover?   I would like to propose that we switch it all at once. This will provide the least disruption in the tracking of performance over time & commits.  There would be a single commit where any change is due to the spmatrix -> sparray change. Trends outside of that commit will be largely still easy to identify.

But we'd like to hear from people to use the benchmark suite. How would you handle the switch? Do you expect to the change to negatively affect what you use the suite for? What would minimize that impact?

Is there any concern about changing the benchmark from spmatrix to sparray all at once and say, before release of 1.18?

title: `integrate.quad`: missing `elif` in error-6 handler causes first validation message to be silently overwritten
body:
In `scipy/integrate/_quadpack_py.py`, the error-6 (invalid input) handler for weighted `alg` integrations uses two sequential `if` statements instead of `if/elif`:

```python
# _quadpack_py.py, lines 595-599
elif weight.startswith('alg'):  # QAWSE
    if min(wvar) < -1:
        msg = "wvar parameters (alpha, beta) must both be >= -1."
    if b < a:                                    # <-- should be `elif`
        msg = "Integration limits a, b must satistfy a<b."
```

When **both** conditions are true (invalid `wvar` parameters **and** `b < a`), the first `msg` assignment is silently overwritten by the second. The caller receives only the integration-limits message and has no way to know the `wvar` parameters were also invalid.

**Impact:**
- Misleading diagnostics when a user passes both an invalid `wvar` and reversed limits
- The error about `wvar` is lost entirely — the user may fix the limits, rerun, and only then discover the wvar issue
- The typo in the existing message (`"satistfy"` → `"satisfy"`) suggests this code path receives limited review

**Questions:**
1. Should these be `elif` chains so only the first matched condition is reported?
2. Or would it be better to collect all violations and report them together?
3. Is there a test covering the double-invalid case for `alg`-weighted integration?

File: `scipy/integrate/_quadpack_py.py`, lines 595–599.

title: `integrate.nquad`: `[dict([])] * depth` creates shared dict references, not independent option dicts per integration level
body:
In `scipy/integrate/_quadpack_py.py` at line 1226, the default `opts` construction uses list multiplication:

```python
if opts is None:
    opts = [dict([])] * depth
```

`[obj] * n` in Python creates a list of `n` references to the **same object** — not `n` independent copies. So all integration levels share a single dict:

```python
>>> d = [dict()] * 3
>>> d[0] is d[1]
True
```

Currently the shared dict is passed into `_OptFunc`, which calls it as a function or treats it as a mapping without mutating it, so the bug is latent. However:

1. **Any future modification** to the per-level opts dict inside `_NQuad._call` or `_OptFunc` would corrupt all levels simultaneously and silently.
2. **Defensive copying** (`opts[i].copy()` etc.) elsewhere in the call stack would not help, since all `opts[i]` point to the same object.
3. The fix is straightforward and has no performance cost:

```python
opts = [dict() for _ in range(depth)]
```

**Questions:**
1. Is the shared-reference behaviour intentional (e.g., opts are treated as immutable throughout)?
2. If so, should a comment document this assumption so future contributors don't inadvertently mutate the opts?
3. Would you accept a patch replacing `[dict([])] * depth` with the list-comprehension form for correctness and clarity?

File: `scipy/integrate/_quadpack_py.py`, line 1226.

title: ENH: stats: Pearson Distribution
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

There isn't a implementation of the pearson distribution in scipy. Currently, only the Type III ([pearson3](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.stats.pearson3.html)) pearson distribution has been implemented. Many of the cases reduce to distributions that are already implemented in scipy. However, relating the first four moments of the distribution to the parameters of the pearson distribution can be tricky. 

### Describe the solution you'd like.

It'd be nice to be able to define a pearson RV like
```python
X = pearson(mean, var, skew, kurtosis)
```

### Describe alternatives you've considered.

_No response_

### Additional context (e.g. screenshots, GIFs)

_No response_

title: ENH/BENCH: signal.oaconvolve: performance enhancements
body:
1. The current implementation of `signal.oaconvolve` is often much slower than `signal.convolve` and `signal.fftconvolve` in expected use cases (FIR filtering, large signal len and small kernel len), despite its theoretically better time complexity. 
2. Furthermore, the current benchmarks (https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/19834f90fc422fa68509c421d393efd27a54b9a8/benchmarks/benchmarks/signal.py#L85-L165) do not provide a objective comparison between `signal.convolve`, `signal.fftconvolve`, `signal.oaconvolve` due to different sized inputs. (Of course, `signal.convolve` by default auto chooses the best convolve algorithm so it should always be the fastest). 

Due to the inability to directly compare using the current benchmarks, here's a quick script to directly compare FIR filtering performance for a typical medical ultrasound workload. (`pyfftconv` is my standalone implementation of fftconvolve and oaconvolve using FFTW3 as the FFT backend).

<details>
<summary>Benchmark code</summary>

```py
from timeit import repeat
import numpy as np
import scipy.signal
# import pyfftconv  # https://github.com/kwsp/fftconv


def run_bench(size1: int, size2: int, n_repeats=10, n_runs=1000):
    def _timeit(stmt, scope: dict):
        for _ in range(5):
            eval(stmt, locals=scope)  # warmup

        repeat_us = (
            np.array(repeat(stmt, repeat=n_repeats, number=n_runs, globals=scope))
            / n_runs
            * 1e6
        )
        mean_us = np.mean(repeat_us)
        std_us = np.std(repeat_us)

        print(f"  ({n_runs}x{n_repeats} runs) {stmt}\t{mean_us:.3f} +- {std_us:.3f} us/it")

    x = np.random.random(size1)
    k = np.random.random(size2)
    scipy.signal.oaconvolve(x, k)
    # pyfftconv.oaconvolve(x, k)

    scope = dict(
        x=x,
        k=k,
        np=np,
        scipy=scipy,
        # pyfftconv=pyfftconv,
    )
    _timeit("np.convolve(x, k)         ", scope)
    _timeit("scipy.signal.convolve(x, k)", scope)
    _timeit("scipy.signal.fftconvolve(x,k)", scope)
    _timeit("scipy.signal.oaconvolve(x,k)", scope)
    # _timeit("pyfftconv.convolve(x, k)", scope)
    # _timeit("pyfftconv.oaconvolve(x, k)", scope)


def run_test_case(size1, size2):
    print(f"=== test case ({size1}, {size2}) ===")
    run_bench(size1, size2)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    run_test_case(1664, 65)
    run_test_case(2816, 65)
    run_test_case(2304, 65)
    run_test_case(4352, 65)

``` 
</details>

```
=== test case (1664, 65) ===
  (1000x10 runs) np.convolve(x, k)              14.062 +- 1.493 μs/it
  (1000x10 runs) scipy.signal.convolve(x, k)    16.793 +- 0.131 μs/it
  (1000x10 runs) scipy.signal.fftconvolve(x,k)  29.041 +- 0.124 μs/it
  (1000x10 runs) scipy.signal.oaconvolve(x,k)   47.980 +- 0.184 μs/it
  (1000x10 runs) pyfftconv.convolve(x, k)       7.530 +- 0.041 μs/it
  (1000x10 runs) pyfftconv.oaconvolve(x, k)     5.564 +- 0.042 μs/it
=== test case (2816, 65) ===
  (1000x10 runs) np.convolve(x, k)              21.447 +- 0.052 μs/it
  (1000x10 runs) scipy.signal.convolve(x, k)    25.252 +- 0.122 μs/it
  (1000x10 runs) scipy.signal.fftconvolve(x,k)  35.834 +- 0.087 μs/it
  (1000x10 runs) scipy.signal.oaconvolve(x,k)   49.722 +- 0.282 μs/it
  (1000x10 runs) pyfftconv.convolve(x, k)       13.553 +- 0.044 μs/it
  (1000x10 runs) pyfftconv.oaconvolve(x, k)     8.787 +- 0.039 μs/it
=== test case (2304, 65) ===
  (1000x10 runs) np.convolve(x, k)              17.689 +- 0.045 μs/it
  (1000x10 runs) scipy.signal.convolve(x, k)    21.415 +- 0.041 μs/it
  (1000x10 runs) scipy.signal.fftconvolve(x,k)  33.250 +- 0.073 μs/it
  (1000x10 runs) scipy.signal.oaconvolve(x,k)   47.732 +- 0.357 μs/it
  (1000x10 runs) pyfftconv.convolve(x, k)       39.357 +- 0.099 μs/it
  (1000x10 runs) pyfftconv.oaconvolve(x, k)     7.764 +- 0.071 μs/it
=== test case (4352, 65) ===
  (1000x10 runs) np.convolve(x, k)              34.455 +- 2.912 μs/it
  (1000x10 runs) scipy.signal.convolve(x, k)    37.760 +- 0.311 μs/it
  (1000x10 runs) scipy.signal.fftconvolve(x,k)  49.822 +- 0.767 μs/it
  (1000x10 runs) scipy.signal.oaconvolve(x,k)   54.853 +- 0.466 μs/it
  (1000x10 runs) pyfftconv.convolve(x, k)       45.777 +- 0.121 μs/it
  (1000x10 runs) pyfftconv.oaconvolve(x, k)     12.342 +- 0.056 μs/it
```

As seen above, `oaconvolve` is currently the slowest implementation from smaller to larger signal sizes. This is likely because the entire "overlap-add" part is implemented in Python. https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/19834f90fc422fa68509c421d393efd27a54b9a8/scipy/signal/_signaltools.py#L842-L1049

I've previously demonstrated the fastest C++ overlap-add convolve implementation (https://github.com/kwsp/fftconv) I could find (outperforming Intel IPP and Apple Accelerate) using FFTW3 as the FFT backend.

For SciPy, I propose to 

1. Fix the benchmark code such that there can be an apples to apples comparison between `signal.convolve`, `signal.fftconvolve`, and `signal.oaconvolve`
1. Enhance `signal.oaconvolve` by implement the overlap-add logic completely in C++ (basically porting https://github.com/kwsp/fftconv from FFTW3 to SciPy's PocketFFT backend).

title: ENH: signal.sosfilt to accept multiple sos arrays
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

When computing the SRS of an acceleration signal, each SDOF can be represented as a biquad filter and thus a single second order section. Currently, it is not possible to provide multiple sos arrays to the function sosfilt.

### Describe the solution you'd like.

I would be a handy addition to make it possible for sosfilt to accept multiple sos arrays (of shape (n_sos, n_section, 6)) and apply them to the input signal(s) independenty (so the output would be (n_sos, n_signal, signal_size)).

### Describe alternatives you've considered.

Currently, I am iterating over the sos arrays, but for a large spectrum, I think that it is not the most efficient way of doing this.

### Additional context (e.g. screenshots, GIFs)

_No response_

title: BUG: scipy.stats.poisson_binom vectorization does not work for >64 inputs

### Describe your issue.

At least two methods in the `scipy.stats.poisson_binom` module do not work for large problem sizes due to hard-coded vectorization limits. 

Description of a Minimum Working Example: 
I create a list of _N_ random probabilities. I then use the `scipy.stats.poisson_binom` module to compute and output the mean, median, variance and a 95% confidence interval. For _N_=61, the output is as expected. For _N_=62, the `scipy.stats.poisson_binom.median()` and `scipy.stats.poisson_binom.interval()` routines fail with identical errors. 

I am using Numpy 2.4.3 (latest), SciPy 1.17.1 (latest) and Python 3.14.3 (latest stable?), all installed via Homebrew 5.1.0 installed on macOS 14.6. 

I have searched for any mention of this issue in the release notes or in the list of known issues but it does not appear to be reported yet. 

Thanks to the devs for making awesome software and for considering this bug report! 

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
from scipy.stats import poisson_binom, uniform

# Input Parameters
confidence_level = 0.95
N = 62

# Generate dummy probabilities
ps = uniform.rvs(loc=0, scale=1, size=N)
print(f"Probabilities: {ps}")

variance = poisson_binom.var( ps )  # Works as expected!
print(f"Variance = {variance}")

print(f"Mean = {mean}")

median = poisson_binom.median( ps )  # FAILS when N>61

interval = poisson_binom.interval( confidence_level, ps, loc=mean )   # FAILS when N>61
```

title: DOC: `elementwise.find_root`: transforming a callable to be strictly elementwise
body:
### Describe your issue.

`elementwise.find_root` allows that `args` which are not broadcastable with `x` can be inherited by `f` from outer scope, stating "If the callable for which the root is desired requires arguments that are not broadcastable with x, wrap that callable with f such that f accepts only x and broadcastable *args."

My challenge is to find the roots of a function`f` of a single variable defined as follows:

f(x): R -> R
f = Sum_i^N g(x;, p_i)

where p_i are parameters for a function g:R->R, such as a polynomial. The number of terms in the sum for `f`, N, must be variable but is fixed for each optimization.

Broadcasting rules in `find_root` require that `g` returns a vector of the same shape as all of its inputs. Since `p` is an array of size `N, len(x)` I could only get the `find_root` to get past initialization by defining `f` to inherit `p` from its outer scope. Broadcasting `x` to `N, len(x)` changes the underlying function `g`.  

When `find_root` iterates, some elements of f(x) converge faster than other elements, and `find_root` maintains a list `active` that is used to slice the variable `x` to avoid further iteration on elements that are converged. The *problem* is that `find_root` doesn't provide a means for the callable function `f` to slice any parameters inherited from its outer scope. Consequently, when one element converges, `x` is no longer broadcastable with `p` and the calculation fails in my function.

I hope my explanation is clear. I think my problem is in scope for `find_root` but I could be misunderstanding the statement about wrapping the callable. If it isn't in scope, maybe the documentation can elaborate a bit to explain why this problem is out of scope.


### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
from scipy.optimize import elementwise


def _g(x, p0, p1, p2):
    return 1. - p0*x**p1 - p2


# test parameters
# scipy requires (neq, nconditions) since it will convert 1d to row vectors
# and xmin, xmax relate to conditions
p0 = np.array([[1, 1],
               [1, 1],
               [1, 1]]).T

p1 = np.array([[5, 5], 
               [5, 5],
               [1, 1]]).T

p2 = np.array([[0, 0], 
               [0, 0],
               [0, 0]]).T


neq, ncond = p0.shape

# inherit p from outer scope
def f(x):
    fx = _g(x, p0, p1, p2).sum(axis=0)
    return fx


# bounds
xmin = np.full((ncond,), 0.)
xmax = np.full((ncond,), 3.)

# check that f computes and sign(f) changes in (xmin, xmax)
print(f(xmin))
print(f(xmax))

# find roots - will fail because position 2 converges before 0, 1
result = elementwise.find_root(f, (xmin, xmax))
```

### Error message

```shell
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
ValueError                                Traceback (most recent call last)
```

title: RFC: declaring netcdf IO functions in `scipy.io` as legacy
body:
There are currently 12 open issues for our `netcdf` IO functions: see [this list](http://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues?q=is%3Aissue%20state%3Aopen%20netcdf%20label%3Ascipy.io).

Some of them are almost a decade old. `scipy.io` is de facto under maintained. In https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/22536 a link to the much better and actively maintained [netcdf4python](https://github.com/Unidata/netcdf4-python) project was added to help users as our netcdf support is very unlikely to improve in future.

I propose to go one step further and officially declare our netcdf functions legacy. scipy cannot compete with netcdf4python and we should not try to reimplement functionality that is well maintained elsewhere already. We can keep `netcdf_file` and `netcdf_variable` around just like `fftpack` without updates, so we are not breaking downstream in the short run.

The legacy decleration would push users to a better alternative and help to focus scipy on its core strengths: fundamental mathematical algorithms and data structures.

title: ENH: interpolate.RBFInterpolator: add Matérn kernel
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

Would a PR to add the python and pythran code required to for a [Matérn kernel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Matérn_covariance_function) be acceptable welcome?

I think this could potentially require and additional kwarg or using **kwargs to pass the value of $\nu$  from RBFInterpolator to the Matern kernel.

Related to #17294 I'm not sure what the status of using the `LowLevelCallable` with pythran is but my first approach would be to add the required pythran compatible code. 

### Describe the solution you'd like.

Add pythran code for Matern's half integer kernels.

### Describe alternatives you've considered.

Passing/using `LowLevel` callable with pythran

### Additional context (e.g. screenshots, GIFs)


This is used in a few paper in my field and I'm sure more broadly so might be useful to others too e.g. [DOI:10.3847/1538-4357/ac158d](https://www.doi.org/10.3847/1538-4357/ac158d) and [DOI:10.3847/1538-4365/acf669](https://www.doi.org/10.3847/1538-4365/acf669)

title: BUG: `_batched_linalg` wrapper for `linalg.solve` silently copies the matrix for C-contiguous arrays
body:
### Describe your issue.

The `_batched_linalg` wrapper in scipy version 1.17+ of `linalg.solve` changed the behavior of `scipy.linalg.solve` for C-contiguous arrays, where the full matrix is copied, and the `overwrite_a=True` argument is silently ignored. I noticed this when running the solver for large matrices, and after updating the scipy version, they started suddenly running into OOM errors.

I tested this with the script below both with a newer scipy version (1.17.1) and older (1.16.3) to see that in the newer case the memory gets duplicated, while in the older it doesn't for the C-contiguous array case. I understand that this change is because LAPACK operates with F-contiguous arrays, so in version 1.17+ the `overwrite_a` argument is silently set to False if the array is C-contiguous. 

However, since this can break functionality due to an OOM error for the user, I would either suggest to have it do the same behavior as before for versions <1.17, where a single F-contiguous copy is made internally, honoring the `overwite_a=True` argument, or to give a warning for the user in the case of a C-contiguous array.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import subprocess
import sys

SIZE = 15000  # 1.8 GB matrix


def run_solver(name: str, code: str) -> None:
    """Run solver in subprocess and print memory usage."""
    full_code = f"""
import gc, resource
import numpy as np

def maxrss_gb():
    return resource.getrusage(resource.RUSAGE_SELF).ru_maxrss / 1e6

n = {SIZE}
np.random.seed(42)

# Create positive definite matrix (A @ A.T creates a temp copy, this will show up in the memory footprint)
A = np.random.randn(n, n)
A = A @ A.T
A += n * np.eye(n)  # In-place add
b = np.random.randn(n)
gc.collect()

baseline = maxrss_gb()

{code}

peak = maxrss_gb()
overhead = peak - baseline
print(f"{{baseline:.2f}},{{peak:.2f}},{{overhead:.2f}}")
"""
    result = subprocess.run(
        [sys.executable, "-c", full_code], capture_output=True, text=True
    )
    if result.returncode != 0:
        print(f"{name:25s}  ERROR: {result.stderr[:80]}")
        return
    try:
        baseline, peak, overhead = result.stdout.strip().split(",")
        print(
            f"{name:25s}  baseline={baseline}GB  peak={peak}GB  overhead={overhead}GB"
        )
    except:
        print(f"{name:25s}  ERROR parsing: {result.stdout[:80]}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    import scipy

    print(f"scipy version: {scipy.__version__}")
    matrix_gb = SIZE**2 * 8 / 1e9
    print(f"Matrix: {SIZE}x{SIZE} = {matrix_gb:.2f} GB\n")

    run_solver("numpy", "x = np.linalg.solve(A, b)")

    run_solver(
        "scipy (C-contiguous)",
        """
import scipy.linalg
x = scipy.linalg.solve(A, b, assume_a='pos', overwrite_a=True)
""",
    )

    run_solver(
        "scipy (F-contiguous)",
        """
import scipy.linalg
A_f = np.asfortranarray(A)  # Extra copy here
x = scipy.linalg.solve(A_f, b, assume_a='pos', overwrite_a=True)
""",
    )

    run_solver(
        "lapack direct",
        """
from scipy.linalg import lapack
potrf, = lapack.get_lapack_funcs(('potrf',), (A,))
potrs, = lapack.get_lapack_funcs(('potrs',), (A,))
c, _ = potrf(A, lower=False, overwrite_a=True)
x, _ = potrs(c, b, lower=False)
""",
    )
```

### Error message

```shell
scipy version: 1.17.1
Matrix: 15000x15000 = 1.80 GB

numpy                      baseline=3.60GB  peak=3.62GB  overhead=0.02GB
scipy (C-contiguous)       baseline=3.60GB  peak=5.44GB  overhead=1.84GB
scipy (F-contiguous)       baseline=3.60GB  peak=3.84GB  overhead=0.24GB
lapack direct              baseline=3.60GB  peak=3.68GB  overhead=0.08GB
```

title: BUG: ValueError: could not convert integer scalar when assigning values to large sparse matrices
body:
### Describe your issue.

Hi everyone, and thanks in advance for your help!

I am encountering a very similar issue as the one described in #24339, and I believe they might be related. I have traced it down to **value assignment** in large sparse matrices.

Essentially, `ValueError: could not convert integer scalar` is raised when assigning values to sparse matrices selecting more than `46340` values in each dimension.

Note: this does not happen when using `lil_matrix`, which I am now using as an intermediate step to work around the issue.

I am including a minimal example to reproduce this below.

Thank you!
Kai

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
import scipy.sparse

print(scipy.__version__)

d = 47000
csr_a = scipy.sparse.csr_matrix((d, d), dtype=np.float32)

# works
csr_a[np.arange(0, 46340)[:, None], np.arange(0, 46340)] = 1

# fails
csr_a[np.arange(0, 46341)[:, None], np.arange(0, 46341)] = 1
```

title: ENH: recently-published Hausdorff algorithms
body:
Ryu and Kamata (2021), https://doi.org/10.1016/j.patcog.2021.107857, published a new directed Hausdorff distance algorithm that they claim outperforms early break Hausdorff distance (EBHD), and our current implementation is from the 2015 EBHD paper/algo they claim to outperform.

Some things that would be useful to do here:

1) Look for low hanging fruit from their paper we could use to get some small speedups without a full rewrite.
2) Make sure our benchmarks match the various scenarios they consider (probably missing a few cases likes high-overlap scenarios).
3) If we find evidence of substantial speedups, consider either replacing our algorithm or offering an argument to swap algorithms used to avoid the scenario where EBHD is still in demand for some specific scenarios.

title: BUG: scipy.integrate.ode with integrator='vode' uses transposed Jacobian convention in 1.17.x
BUG: The COBYLA optimizer doesn't seem to respect boundaries
body:
### Describe your issue.

I'm using the `scipy.optimize.minimize` function to optimize [marine propellers](https://github.com/GijsB/propy). The default SLSQP optimizer has been working fine for a while, but you know, the grass is always greener on the other side. 

When using the `COBYLA` method, the optimizer attempts to calculate the objective- (and constraint-) function with arguments that are outside of the set bounds. This is not allowed for my specific objective/constraint functions, so I'd like to prevent the optimizer from attempting this working point. My naive expectation of this method is that this would be possible, given that `COBYLA` is mentioned in the `bounds` keyword in the documentation:

> Bounds on variables for Nelder-Mead, L-BFGS-B, TNC, SLSQP, Powell, trust-constr, COBYLA, and COBYQA methods. 

I'm sorry for the spam if this is simply expected behavior! Thank you in advance for your help!

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
from scipy.optimize import minimize, Bounds

lb = (0.03, 0.3, 0.5)
x0 = (1.0, 0.5, 0.8)
ub = (30, 1.05, 1.4)

def f(args):
    print(f'Objective({args})')
    for l, u, x in zip(lb, ub, args):
        if not l <= x <= u:
            raise RuntimeError(f'Argument out of bounds, {l} <= {x} <= {u}')

    return sum(x**2 for x in args)



opt_res = minimize(
    method='COBYLA',
    fun=f,
    x0=x0,
    bounds=Bounds(
        lb=lb,
        ub=ub,
        keep_feasible=(True, True, True)
    ),
    options = {
        'disp': 3
    }
)
```

### Error message

title: BUG: ABI-breaking changes for `cython_special.bdtrik` & co in `main`
body:
Opening an issue now that I discovered this. I'm still in the middle of adding comprehensive tests after a similar ABI break in `linalg`. The tests I'm about to add will show this:
```
E           Failed: scipy.special.cython_special.__pyx_capi__ has 4 ABI breakage(s):
E           REMOVED  'bdtrik' (was 'double (double, double, double, int __pyx_skip_dispatch)')
E           REMOVED  'bdtrin' (was 'double (double, double, double, int __pyx_skip_dispatch)')
E           REMOVED  'nbdtrik' (was 'double (double, double, double, int __pyx_skip_dispatch)')
E           REMOVED  'nbdtrin' (was 'double (double, double, double, int __pyx_skip_dispatch)')
```

These are caused by changed to `cython_special.pxd`, e.g. in gh-24112. 

I'll follow up soon, just marking this as a potential blocker for 1.18.0.

title: CI: linalg: dev deps failing due to NumPy deprecation of generic time units
body:
### Describe your issue.

In https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/29619, NumPy was changed to warn when users created a timedelta value which did not specify the units of time.

This creates several errors inside the scipy/linalg/tests/test_basic.py::TestDTypes test, which tests various linear algebra operations against all dtypes.

```python
spin test -s linalg -- -k TestDTypes
```

title: BUG: QMC Sobol' sequences may violate the (0,m,2)-net property for dimension >= 3
body: ### Describe your issue. This is a question about sequences generated by `scipy.stats.qmc.Sobol()`. My understanding of the (0,m,2)-net property is that each elementary interval of volume 2^{-m} should have exactly one point in it from a sequence of 2^{m} points. See, e.g., the discussion near the beginning here: https://arxiv.org/abs/2008.08051 This property seems to be met by 2D Sobol' sequences generated by `qmc.Sobol()`. However, as best I can tell, when I generate a sequence in 3 dimensions, some of the dimension pairs do not satisfy this property. See below - the combinations of x0 and x1 seem to have one point in each box, but there are several empty boxes in x0 vs. x2. Is something wrong or is there something in the definition that I am misunderstanding? (If the latter, many apologies for bothering you!) ### Reproducing Code Example ```python from scipy.stats import qmc from matplotlib import pyplot as plt sampler = qmc.Sobol(d=3, scramble=False) samples = sampler.random_base2(m=5) indices = [(0,1),(0,2)] #dimension pairs powers = [(2,3),(2,3)] #log2 of number of splits (sum should be <= m above) lo,hi = 0,1 fig, axs = plt.subplots(1,2,figsize=(10,5)) for (ax,(i1,i2),(m1,m2)) in zip(axs,indices,powers): ax.scatter(samples[:,i1],samples[:,i2]) #add vertical lines for i in range(1,2**m1): ax.axvline(x=lo+(hi-lo)*i/(2**m1),c='k',linestyle='--') #add horizontal lines for i in range(1,2**m2): ax.axhline(y=lo+(hi-lo)*i/(2**m2),c='k',linestyle='--') ax.set_title(f'x{i1} vs. x{i2}, splits=({2**m1},{2**m2})') ax.set_aspect('equal', 'box') ax.set_xlim(lo,hi) ax.set_ylim(lo,hi) plt.show() ``` ### Error message ```shell N/A ``` ### SciPy/NumPy/Python version and system information title: BUG: overflow in `ndimage._measurements._select`
body:
### Describe your issue.

`ndimage._measurements._select` computes `labels.max() + 2` to allocate index arrays:
https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/527eb7fd7953a1de068f94bf8b322f249b9405ae/scipy/ndimage/_measurements.py#L1014
but the addition is performed in the label array's dtype. When `labels` is `uint8` with `max() >= 254` (or `uint16` with `max() >= 65534`), this overflows to 0, creating a zero-length array that causes an `IndexError`.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
from scipy import ndimage

labels = np.zeros(256, dtype=np.uint8)
labels[0] = 254
values = np.ones(256, dtype=np.float32)
ndimage.median(values, labels, index=[254])
```

### Error message

```shell
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
IndexError                                Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[2], line 7
      5 labels[0] = 254
      6 values = np.ones(256, dtype=np.float32)
----> 7 ndimage.median(values, labels, index=[254])

File ~/src/venv-3.12/lib/python3.12/site-packages/scipy/ndimage/_measurements.py:1239, in median(input, labels, index)
   1180 def median(input, labels=None, index=None):
   1181     """
   1182     Calculate the median of the values of an array over labeled regions.
   1183 
   (...)   1237 
   1238     """
-> 1239     return _select(input, labels, index, find_median=True)[0]

File ~/src/venv-3.12/lib/python3.12/site-packages/scipy/ndimage/_measurements.py:1015, in _select(input, labels, index, find_min, find_max, find_min_positions, find_max_positions, find_median)
   1013 locs = np.arange(len(labels))
   1014 lo = np.zeros(labels.max() + 2, np.int_)
-> 1015 lo[labels[::-1]] = locs[::-1]
   1016 hi = np.zeros(labels.max() + 2, np.int_)
   1017 hi[labels] = locs

IndexError: index 0 is out of bounds for axis 0 with size 0
```

title: BUG: linalg: quirky behavior of `overwrite_x` with batches and f2py
body:
### Describe your issue.

When working on re-enabling the `overwrite_x` arguments, it was discovered that the combination of f2py, the batching operator and the `overwrite_x` arguments can be abused to get completely wrong outputs. See also #24645 and #24963 for some potentially relevant background information.

At first glance it seems like the problem stems from the following: the batching decorator makes it such that f2py is always fed with one slice, which then checks whether the input array is F-ordered, and if so overwrites the output if requested. However, if the input array has (partially) overlapping slices, e.g. due to broadcasting and/or stride tricks, this means that the computations in one slice will alter the inputs of the next slice, leading to wrong outputs. See also the MWE below, copied from #24963. (In fact, IIRC this is one of the main reasons why it was decided in #24442 to, at least for now, only allow `overwrite_x` for 2D arrays as it is non-trivial to identify all cases in which stride tricks would or would not cause problems here.)

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
import scipy as sp

rng = np.random.default_rng(seed=42)
a = np.triu(rng.normal(size=(2, 10, 10)))
b = rng.normal(size=(1, 5, 10)).transpose(0, 2, 1) # ensure F-ordered
b_ref = np.copy(b)

x_true = sp.linalg.solve_triangular(a, b, overwrite_b=False)
x_overwrite = sp.linalg.solve_triangular(a, b, overwrite_b=True)

assert np.allclose(a @ x_true, b_ref)
assert np.allclose(x_overwrite[0, :, :], x_overwrite[1, :, :])
assert not np.allclose(a @ x_overwrite, b_ref)
```

title: BUG: sparse.coo_matrix.sum: does not interpret `dtype` as documented with duplicate values
body:
### Describe your issue.

This is a follow-up to issue #23768 and PR #24496, which fixed the `dtype` casting bug for CSR/CSC arrays and matrices. The same bug still exists in COO sparse matrices when they contain duplicate entries.

Unlike CSR/CSC, COO explicitly permits duplicate entries as a documented advantage. The `sum()` method documentation states: "The type of the returned array/matrix and of the accumulator in which the elements are summed." This indicates `dtype` should affect the accumulator (cast-before), but the current implementation uses cast-after behavior

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
from scipy.sparse import coo_matrix

row = np.array([0, 0, 1])
col = np.array([0, 0, 1])
data = np.array([0.6, 0.6, 1.2])
m = coo_matrix((data, (row, col)), shape=(3, 2))
print(m.sum(axis=0, dtype=int))
```

### Error message

```shell
# expected [[0 1]], obtained [[1 1]]
# Note: This bug does not occur for COO matrices without duplicate entries
```

### SciPy/NumPy/Python version and system information

```shell
1.18.0.dev0+git20260309.d30677e 2.4.3 sys.version_info(major=3, minor=12, micro=3, releaselevel='final', serial=0)
```

### Tool-Use Disclosure
This bug was identified by an automated testing tool currently being developed by our research team. 
Before reporting it, we reviewed the code and checked the latest development version of SciPy to confirm the issue.

title: ENH: signal.whittaker_henderson: add GCV criterion
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

I'd like the automatic penalty selection of Whittaker-Henderson smoothing to use the GCV criterion. Currently, REML is implemented.

See, for example:
Weinert (2007)
    "Efficient computation for Whittaker-Henderson smoothing".
    Computational Statistics and Data Analysis 52:959-74.
    https://doi.org/10.1016/j.csda.2006.11.038

### Describe the solution you'd like.

```py
whittaker_henderson(signal, lamb="GCV")
```

### Describe alternatives you've considered.

_No response_

### Additional context (e.g. screenshots, GIFs)

_No response_

title: ENH: signal.whittaker_henderson: add support to smooth 2-dimensional signals
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

Currently, `whittaker_henderson(signal)` only supports 1-dimensional arrays `signal`. There are important applications where the signal i 2-dimensional or even 3-dimensional.

See https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.06932

### Describe the solution you'd like.

Support 2-dim signals. The tricky part is the extension of the penalty, see https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.06932.

### Describe alternatives you've considered.

_No response_

### Additional context (e.g. screenshots, GIFs)

_No response_

title: ENH: scipy.linalg.dsymv — optimal symmetric matrix-vector product with proven n(n+1)/2 bilinear complexity
body:
## Is your feature request related to a problem?

`scipy.linalg` wraps LAPACK/BLAS but does not expose `DSYMV` or `DSYMM` in a
way that guarantees the symmetric structure is exploited at the multiplication
level. Calling `scipy.linalg.blas.dsymv` dispatches to the vendor BLAS, which
may or may not exploit symmetry internally — and most don't at the scalar
multiplication level.

The result: a 256×256 symmetric matrix-vector product costs 65,536
multiplications everywhere in the NumPy/SciPy stack, when the proven lower
bound is 32,896.

## Describe the solution you'd like:

A `scipy.linalg.dsymv(A, v)` function that achieves the proven-optimal
bilinear complexity of **n(n+1)/2 multiplications** for symmetric A.

A working reference implementation is on PyPI:

```
pip install khalgebra
```

```python
import scipy.linalg
import khalgebra as kh

n = 512
A = kh.make_sym_mat(n)
v = kh.make_vec(n)

w_ref = scipy.linalg.blas.dsymv(1.0, A, v)  # vendor BLAS, symmetry not guaranteed
w_opt = kh.khal_dsymv(A, v)                  # n(n+1)/2 mults, proven optimal

kh.max_abs_err(w_ref, w_opt)  # < 1e-12
```

Multiplication savings:

| n | current (n²) | optimal n(n+1)/2 | savings |
|---|---|---|---|
| 64 | 4,096 | 2,080 | 49% |
| 256 | 65,536 | 32,896 | 50% |
| 1,024 | 1,048,576 | 524,800 | 50% |

Proof of optimality: Khalil 2025. Correctness verified n=2…1024, max error < 1e-9.

Describe alternatives you've considered:

`scipy.linalg.blas.dsymv` exists but delegates to the vendor BLAS with no
guarantee of optimal multiplication count. The khalgebra implementation shows
the optimal algorithm is straightforward to implement in pure Python/NumPy —
it does not require a custom C extension.

Open source code and benchmarks can be found in repo https://github.com/MahmoodKhalil57/khalgebra

title: BUG: integrate.cubature: `test_result_dtype_promoted_correctly` specifies rtol smaller than machine epsilon
body:
### Describe your issue.

There is a test inside the jax CPU CI test which is very slow, `test_result_dtype_promoted_correctly`.

This test numerically integrates the function `f(x) = x**1` over the range [0, 1]. It calls cubature three times: once with float64 inputs, once with float32 inputs, and once with mixed inputs. The call we are concerned with is the second call, with float32 inputs.

The default rtol for cubature is 1e-8. This test does not specify an rtol.

On numpy, it takes 87 subdivisions when using an rtol of 1e-8.

On JAX and pytorch, this uses 10,000 subdivisions, the maximum number. It does not converge.

This seems like it is either a bug in `cubature()`, in that its subdivision behavior varies so much between numpy and jax/pytorch, or an issue with the test, in that it should not specify an rtol below the machine epsilon of 1.19e-07.

### Reproducing Code Example

Test command

```bash
spin test --build-dir=build-cpu -m 'array_api_backends and not slow' -s integrate -b jax.numpy -- --durations 3 --timeout=120 -k test_result_dtype_promoted_correctly -s
```

Modification of test to print more debugging info

```diff
-        result_dtype = cubature(
+        result = cubature(
             basic_1d_integrand,
             xp.asarray([0], dtype=xp.float64),
             xp.asarray([1], dtype=xp.float64),
             points=[],
             args=(xp.asarray([1], dtype=xp.float64), xp),
-        ).estimate.dtype
+        )
+        result_dtype = result.estimate.dtype
+        print("float64")
+        print(f"{len(result.regions)=}")
+        print(f"{result.subdivisions=}")
 
         assert result_dtype == xp.float64
 
-        result_dtype = cubature(
+        result = cubature(
             basic_1d_integrand,
             xp.asarray([0], dtype=xp.float32),
             xp.asarray([1], dtype=xp.float32),
             points=[],
             args=(xp.asarray([1], dtype=xp.float32), xp),
-        ).estimate.dtype
+        )
+        result_dtype = result.estimate.dtype
+        print("float32")



+        print(f"{len(result.regions)=}")
+        print(f"{result.subdivisions=}")
 
         assert result_dtype == xp.float32
 
-        result_dtype = cubature(
+        result = cubature(
             basic_1d_integrand,
             xp.asarray([0], dtype=xp.float32),
             xp.asarray([1], dtype=xp.float64),
             points=[],
             args=(xp.asarray([1], dtype=xp.float32), xp),
-        ).estimate.dtype
+        )
+        result_dtype = result.estimate.dtype
+        print("mixed")
+        print(f"{len(result.regions)=}")
+        print(f"{result.subdivisions=}")
 
         assert result_dtype == xp.float64
```

### Error message

```shell
float32
```

title: DOC: `scipy.interpolate.interpn` with method='linear' accepts length-one axes
body:
### Describe your issue.

Thanks for maintaining scipy! 

I noticed a potential ambiguity in `scipy.interpolate.interpn` when using `method="linear"` with a grid that contains an axis length = 1.

In the example below, the function accepts the input and returns a result, even though linear interpolation typically relies on having at least two grid points per axis to form interpolation intervals.

It is unclear from the documentation if this case is supported or if it should raise an error or warning. 


### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
from scipy.interpolate import interpn

points = (np.array([0.0]), np.array([0.0, 1.0]))  # first axis has length 1
values = np.array([[0.0, 1.0]]) 
xi = np.array([[0.0, 0.0]])
res = interpn(points, values, xi, method='linear', bounds_error=True)
print(res)
```

```shell
[0.] # The result
```

title: DOC: replace broken UiO spline notes references in `scipy.interpolate`
body:
### Issue with current documentation:

Some spline-related references in `scipy.interpolate` still point to old UiO lecture notes from INF-MAT5340 -- Spline Methods, however that course was discontinued a while ago and replaced with MAT4170 -- Spline Methods. As such, the old lecture notes are not easily available, and the existing URL returns a 404.

The affected docs are atleast
- https://scipy.github.io/devdocs/reference/generated/scipy.interpolate.BSpline.html
- https://scipy.github.io/devdocs/reference/generated/scipy.interpolate.BSpline.from_power_basis.html

### Idea or request for content:

I think it would it make sense to replace it with either:

- an archived version of the original, e.g.:
  https://web.archive.org/web/20221205112612/https://www.uio.no/studier/emner/matnat/ifi/nedlagte-emner/INF-MAT5340/v05/undervisningsmateriale/hele.pdf

- or the newer 2025 UiO spline notes:
  https://www.uio.no/studier/emner/matnat/math/MAT4170/v25/undervisningsmateriale/spline_notes.pdf

The newer notes contain much of the same material, so either updating to the archived original or switching to the current course notes would fix the broken reference. I also believe Professor Michael Floater, who is currently teaching the spline course, is planning on turning the new notes into an actual text book, so I believe that would be the more stable reference.

### Additional context (e.g. screenshots, GIFs)

_No response_

title: BUG: `scipy.sparse.linalg.svds` rng argument issue when using `numpy <2.2`
body:
### Describe your issue.

Hello,

In `scipy.sparse.linalg.svds`, the `rng` argument may be provided as a `np.random.RandomState`, which is used to initialise an rng _via_ `np.random.default_rng`.

This is done in the `_iv` input validation function here:
https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/8c75ae75176236f233824e9a0483c26a69e6dfec/scipy/sparse/linalg/_eigen/_svds.py#L91-L94

However, the functionality allowing `np.random.default_rng` to take `np.random.RandomState` was only introduced in `numpy==2.2.0` (in this PR: https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27771). My example below errors with `numpy==2.1.3` but doesn't with `numpy==2.2.0`.

This means `svds` incompatible with older versions of `numpy` if called in this way.

(If you look at the input validation naively, you'd expect other types like _e.g._ `rng=0` to also fail, but `svds` is decorated with `@_transition_to_rng`, which calls `np.random.default_rng`, so we don't end up hitting the issue)

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
from scipy.sparse.linalg import svds
import numpy as np

_ = svds(np.eye(2), k=1)  # note this executes fine
_ = svds(np.eye(2), k=1, rng=np.random.RandomState(0))  # this fails
```

### Error message


title: BUG: `cython_lapack` signatures: `bint` variables become `int64_t` in the ILP64 build
body:
### Describe your issue.

`cython_lapack` uses the `bint` type for Fotran `logical` variables, for example `*gees` (here, the second-to-last `bwork` argument):

```
$ cat cython_lapack.pxd |grep dgees
cdef void dgees(char *jobvs, char *sort, dselect2 *select, int *n, d *a, int *lda, int *sdim, d *wr, d *wi, d *vs, int *ldvs, d *work, int *lwork, bint *bwork, int *info) noexcept nogil
```

In the ILP64-enabled build with `-Dcython-blas-abi=ilp64`, these variables become `int64_t`:

```
cdef void dgees(char *jobvs, char *sort, dselect2 *select, int64_t *n, d *a, int64_t *lda, int64_t *sdim, d *wr, d *wi, d *vs, int64_t *ldvs, d *work, int64_t *lwork, int64_t *bwork, int64_t *info) noexcept nogil
```

As a result, compiling a user code chokes on type mismatch in either LP64 or ILP64 build. 

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
.
```

### Error message

```shell
statsmodels/tsa/statespace/_tools.pyx:4400:26: Cannot assign type 'bool *' to 'int64_t *'
```

### SciPy/NumPy/Python version and system information

```shell
scipy main branch
```

title: BUG: scipy.stats.invgauss.logsf returns NaN for valid small-mu inputs
body:
### Describe your issue.

`scipy.stats.invgauss.logsf` returns `nan` for valid inputs with small `mu`, and `sf` also returns `nan`.

According to the SciPy docs, `sf` is the survival function and `logsf` is the log of the survival function. My input had valid inverse Gaussian parameters (`x > 0`, `mu > 0`), so `sf` should be a probability in `[0, 1]`, and `logsf` should be finite and non-positive or `-inf` if the probability underflows. It should not return `nan`.

This may be related to the older small-`mu` inverse Gaussian issue here: https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/13614

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
import scipy
from scipy.stats import invgauss

mu = 1e-9
x = 8.286427728546843e-06

print("logsf:", invgauss.logsf(x, mu))
print("sf:", invgauss.sf(x, mu))
```

### Error message

```shell
❯ python invgauss_issue_repro.py
/home/user/Projects/scratch/black-scholes/myenv/lib64/python3.14/site-packages/scipy/stats/_continuous_distns.py:5067: RuntimeWarning: invalid value encountered in log1p
  return a + np.log1p(-np.exp(b - a))
logsf: nan
sf: nan
```
