title: ENH: Add high-performance bracketing root-finder (mpbfms)
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

Hi all,

Following up on the discussion in PR #25040  with @dschmitz89, I am opening this ticket to make the case for including a new, efficient deterministic bracketing root-finding algorithm to `scipy.optimize`.

I am proposing the addition of the **Multi-Phase Bisection-False Position-Modified Secant (`mpbfms`)** algorithm. 

### Motivation & performance to Current Methods
Currently, `scipy.optimize.brentq` is the standard choice for reliable, bracketed root finding in SciPy. `mpbfms` combines classical bracketing methods with modified secant refinement to achieve the **exact same absolute guarantee of convergence** as Brent's method, but it is faster and requires significantly fewer iterations across a diverse set of benchmark problems.

**Reference:**
> Ellithy, A. (2026). "Four New Multi-Phase Hybrid Bracketing Algorithms for Numerical Root Finding", Journal of the Egyptian Mathematical Society, 34(1), pp. 91-115. [doi: 10.21608/joems.2026.440115.1078](https://joems.journals.ekb.eg/article_498939.html)

### Executable Benchmarks
To prove its superiority, I have tested `mpbfms` against `brentq` across a standard benchmark suite of 48 diverse functions (with tolerance `1e-14`). 

The reproducible benchmarks, implementation code, and raw results can be found in my repository: [[hybridroots](https://github.com/Abdelrhman-Ellithy/hybridroots/tree/main)](https://github.com/Abdelrhman-Ellithy/hybridroots/tree/main/Paper%20Experiment%20and%20Results).

You can run these benchmarks locally using:
```bash
python -m hybridroots.benchmarks --scipy
```


**Results Summary:**
Over the 48 benchmark problems, `mpbfms` was significantly faster than `brentq` in total CPU time and took roughly a third of the iterations. 

| Algorithm | Converged | Total Time (us) | Avg NFE | Avg Iterations |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `brentq` (SciPy) | 48/48 (100%) | 588.81 | 9.12 | 8.12 |
| `mpbfms` (Proposed) | 48/48 (100%) | **241.77** | 11.65 | **2.69** |

*Note on individual problem wins:* Out of the 48 problems tested, `mpbfms` achieved the fastest CPU time in **22 problems** (compared to 1 win for Brent) and the absolute lowest iteration count in **23 problems**. 

### Implementation Details
* Pure Python, no additional dependencies,  C, C++ version available as well,.
* Shares the exact same interface and method signature as the existing `brentq` and `bisect` functions.
* Includes strict safeguards for numerical stability (division by zero, overflow).

[Algorithm_CPU_Time_us_Matrix.csv](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/27250611/Algorithm_CPU_Time_us_Matrix.csv)
[Algorithm_Iterations_Matrix.csv](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/27250608/Algorithm_Iterations_Matrix.csv)
[Complexity_Per_Algorithm.csv](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/27250609/Complexity_Per_Algorithm.csv)
[CPU_Times_Per_Algorithm.csv](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/27250610/CPU_Times_Per_Algorithm.csv)
[Iterations_Per_Algorithm.csv](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/27250612/Iterations_Per_Algorithm.csv)


@dschmitz89 

### Describe the solution you'd like.

New Algorithm out 4 Algorithms with proven experiments on 48 problems, 10000 iterations, warm up before experiments, performance evaluated on GitHub runners during pipeline.

### Describe alternatives you've considered.

_No response_

### Additional context (e.g. screenshots, GIFs)

[Paper link](https://joems.journals.ekb.eg/article_498939.html)

title: BUG: unnecessary warning before correct answer in scipy.stats.invgauss.ppf
body:
### Describe your issue.

`scipy.stats.invgauss.ppf` often gives the correct answer but only after a warning that it could not locate a solution. For example, if I run `scipy.stats.invgauss.ppf(0.97969, 66.99652081)`
* I get the correct answer `591.5678807399854` but 
* I also get the following warning:
   - `/usr/lib/python3.14/site-packages/scipy/stats/_continuous_distns.py:5078: RuntimeWarning: Error in function  oost::math::quantile(const inverse_gaussian_distribution<d>&, %1%): Unable to locate solution in a reasonable time:  either there is no answer to quantile or the answer is infinite.  Current best guess is %1%   ppf = np.asarray(scu._invgauss_ppf(x, mu, 1))`

I checked that the answer is correct by feeding the answer into the `cdf` function:
* `scipy.stats.invgauss.cdf(591.5678807399854, 66.99652081)` returns `0.9796900000000001` 
* This value differs from the original input to the `ppf` function only in the 16th decimal place.

Moreover, the `qinvgauss` function in the `R` package `statmod` gives the same answer for the `ppf` but without any warning.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
scipy.stats.invgauss.ppf(0.97969, 66.99652081)
scipy.stats.invgauss.cdf(591.5678807399854, 66.99652081)
```

### Error message

```shell
/usr/lib/python3.14/site-packages/scipy/stats/_continuous_distns.py:5078: RuntimeWarning: Error in function  oost::math::quantile(const inverse_gaussian_distribution<d>&, %1%): Unable to locate solution in a reasonable time:  either there is no answer to quantile or the answer is infinite. title: DOC: stats: Ensure all distributions cite a useful reference in a "References" section.
body:
### Issue with current documentation:

When I recently updated the `halfgennorm` distribution, I found it a little frustrating that there was no reference to an online article, published book, or journal paper that described the distribution.  The existing link is to a related distribution, but it does not include the "half" aspect.

I found that many of our distribution docstrings do not have references relevant to the distribution.  We should try to ensure that each distribution has at least one useful reference.  (More is OK, e.g. a book reference and a wikipedia article.)

For the widely used distributions (beta, chi2, gamma, etc.), there will almost certainly be a wikipedia article that we can cite.  Others might be found in one of the books by Johnson, Kotz and Balakrishnan.  Some of the esoteric distributions might require some digging to find the original papers where the distribution was first defined.  Also note that some references might use a different parametrization than that of SciPy. In that case, it would be nice to document the difference in the Notes section.

A quick look at the first few distributions listed at https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/stats.html  shows the following lack references:

* `anglit`
* `arcsine`
* `beta` (Boost reference is not a good main reference.)
* `bradford`
* `cauchy` (Boost reference is not a good main reference.)
* `chi`
* `chi2`
* `cosine`
* `genhalflogistic`
* <strike>`halfgennorm` (The existing reference is for the generalized normal distribution, which is useful, but a reference that includes the "half" aspect of the distribution would be better.)</strike> (As far as I can tell, `halfgennorm` was created by a SciPy developer, and there is no other primary source for this specific distribution.  I tweaked the docstring in https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/25193.) 
* ... etc. I didn't check them all manually.  Below I show the output of a script that looks for distributions with docstrings that are missing the "References" section.

If anyone uses AI to work on this, please be sure to *verify the correctness of any references proposed by AI*.  We don't want any hallucinations sneaking into our docs.  Whether the reference is a book, a journal article or wikipedia, verify that, for example, the PDF shown in the article matches SciPy's PDF.  This might require some algebraic manipulation if the reference doesn't use the same parametrization as that of SciPy.  In this case, it helps our users if we include something in the Notes about how to convert between the SciPy parametrization and that of the reference.


-----

I added a script at https://github.com/WarrenWeckesser/analyze-scipy-code to find distributions with docstrings that are missing a "References" section. Note that a distribution not being in this list doesn't necessarily mean the distribution doesn't need an additional reference or two (e.g. `beta`, `cauchy`).

```

I think there is potential for some docs improvements for `stats.ansari` after that, especially related to JAX/JIT side effects for some argument combinations, as noted over there in my review comments, so opening this ticket to remind us to do that "eventually."

title: BUG: `nct.cdf` return `nan`
body:
### Describe your issue.

Some parameter combinations will cause the `nct.cdf` to return `nan`.

```python
>>> from scipy.stats import nct
>>> nct.cdf(1.9600281895946416, 36949.546033616614, 38.560852472445234)
np.float64(nan) 
```

If you change parameter `x` to `1.9600281895946410`, this issue does not occur.

```python

```

`x = 1.960028189594642` returns `nan` too.

```python

But, if `x = 1.960028189594643`, the function returns the normal result again!

title: MKL minimum supported version
body:
MKL versions below 2025.xx.yy had several misnamed symbols which we had to work around, https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/main/scipy/_build_utils/src/_mkl_ilp64_fixes.h


```
#define cgetc2_64_ cgetc2_64
#define cgtts2_64_ cgtts2_64
#define clarfx_64_ clarfx_64
// ...
```

This was fixed in MKL 2026.0, released 29 Apr 2026, and is available from pypi and conda-forge.

We can thus drop our workarounds if/when we're happy to bump the minimum supported MKL version to 2026.0. 

The patch itself is trivial, https://github.com/scipy/scipy/compare/main...ev-br:mkl_2026?expand=1, so the main question is when we're happy to limit the minimum version (after the 1.18 release for sure).

title: ENH: Add Zernike polynomials to `scipy.special`
body:
I would like to propose to add the Zernike polynomials (radial and circle generators, from Born & Wolf definition) to `scipy.special`. They are essential in fields like optics for aberration study, and also used for modelling galaxy clusters images.

I have some code lying around (from the python package [pyoof](https://github.com/tcassanelli/pyoof)).

title: BUG: Thread-safety of `LinearConstraint.__init__` due to warnings
body:
### Describe your issue.

In `LinearConstraint.__init__`, all warnings are promoted to errors: https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/1629f866cc7ba0301630dd60c6ed272d3938c127/scipy/optimize/_constraints.py#L202-L208

This can lead to issues in multithreaded code as unrelated warnings from other parts of the code may be promoted to errors while this block runs.

Would it be possible to narrow the scope so that it does not impact unrelated code with something like:
```python
simplefilter("error", category=VisibleDeprecationWarning)
```

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import threading
import warnings

import numpy as np
from scipy.optimize import LinearConstraint


def build_constraints():
    A = np.eye(3)
    for _ in range(100000):
        LinearConstraint(A, lb=0.0, ub=1.0)


def emit_unrelated_warnings():
    with warnings.catch_warnings():
        warnings.simplefilter("default")
        for _ in range(100000):
            warnings.warn("unrelated warning", UserWarning)


threads = [
    threading.Thread(target=build_constraints),
    threading.Thread(target=build_constraints),
    threading.Thread(target=emit_unrelated_warnings),
]
for t in threads:
    t.start()
for t in threads:
    t.join()
```

### Error message

title: BUG: stats.median_abs_deviation fails on NaN with Tuple Axis Input
body:
### Describe your issue.

I'm using SciPy's stats.median_abs_deviation to calculate a median on a 3D numpy array. I've noticed that the function fails when there are np.nan's in the 3D array AND I use a tuple in the axis input. Without a tuple axis input, the np.nan's seem to be handled OK.

I was wondering if it was related to this older closed [issue](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/24560)--but I'm not using Pandas objects.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
from scipy import stats
import numpy as np

#Sim a 3D array with no nans
x = np.random.normal(0,1,size=10000)
y = x.reshape(-1,10,10)

#Sim a 3D array with 1 nan
x1 = x.copy()
x1[100] = np.nan
y1 = x1.reshape(-1,10,10)

#This is fine
y_meds = stats.median_abs_deviation(y,axis=(1,2))
print(y_meds)

#This fails with a tuple can't be used as an int error
y1_meds = stats.median_abs_deviation(y1,axis=(1,2),nan_policy='propagate')
#This fails too
y1_meds = stats.median_abs_deviation(y1,axis=(1,2),nan_policy='omit')

#Without the tuple axis input, nan's handled fine
y1_meds = stats.median_abs_deviation(y1,axis=0)
print(y1_meds)
y1_meds = stats.median_abs_deviation(y1,axis=0,nan_policy='omit')
print(y1_meds)
```

### Error message

```shell
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "[user]/lib/python3.12/site-packages/scipy/stats/_stats_py.py", line 3606, in median_abs_deviation
    mad = np.apply_along_axis(_mad_1d, axis, x, center, nan_policy)
          ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "[user]/lib/python3.12/site-packages/numpy/lib/shape_base.py", line 361, in apply_along_axis
    axis = normalize_axis_index(axis, nd)
           ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
TypeError: 'tuple' object cannot be interpreted as an integer
```

title: BUG: signal savgol_filter fails for large >20000 window_length scipy 1.17.1
body:
### Describe your issue.

Earlier versions of scipy had no troubles with properly smoothing the data provided in the reproducing code example. Tracing savgol_filter in scipy 1.17.1 versus scipy 1.13.1 the issue appears related to this line
https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/de6696861c733a427ce3c790bcb662e3589ce5bb/scipy/signal/_polyutils.py#L83
where the matrix conditioning parameter depends on the window size. The conditioning parameter becomes too large for LAPACK to provide valid smoothing coefficients.
scipy 1.13.1 would set 
`cond = np.finfo(lapack_func.dtype).eps`
only independent of the window size.
Change is related to this effort 
https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/23662

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import scipy.signal as sig

if __name__ == '__main__':

    delx = 0.001
    Nsteps = 400000
    x = np.arange(Nsteps)*delx
    period = 120.0
    ysignal = np.cos(x/period*2.0*np.pi)
    noiseAmp = 0.03
    ysignal = ysignal + np.random.normal(0.0, noiseAmp, size=(Nsteps,))
    window = 40.0
    window_n = int(window/delx)
    print(f'Data Size: {len(ysignal)} Window Size: {window_n}')
    y_smooth = sig.savgol_filter(x=ysignal, window_length=window_n, polyorder=3, mode='interp')
    plt.plot(x, ysignal, '.', label='data')
    plt.plot(x, y_smooth, '.', label='Savitzky-Golay Smoothed')
    plt.legend()
    plt.show()
```

### Error message

```shell
No Error message is produced. Functionality of savitzky-golay filter is degraded for large window sizes.
```

title: BUG: Inconsistent definition of `btype` in signal processing
ENH: Expose public distribution base classes and monotonic transform distribution
body:
This issue is in response to a request by @mdhaber on scipy/scipy#24586.

## Summary

I’d like to request that SciPy expose the core distribution classes needed by downstream libraries:
```python
scipy.stats.ContinuousDistribution
scipy.stats.DiscreteDistribution
scipy.stats.MonotonicTransformedDistribution
```
corresponding to the current private infrastructure classes.

I need these for three purposes.

### 1. Type disambiguation

Downstream code needs a public, supported way to distinguish continuous and discrete distributions:
```python
    if isinstance(dist, ContinuousDistribution):
        ...
    elif isinstance(dist, DiscreteDistribution):
        ...
```
Alternatives such as `hasattr`, duck-typing individual members, or defining downstream `Protocol`s are significantly worse. They check for particular attributes, not the whole distribution interface. They also communicate intent less clearly and are more verbose. A base-class check is succinct and expresses the actual semantic distinction: this object is a continuous distribution, or this object is a discrete distribution.

### 2. Type annotations

Downstream libraries also need these classes for reasonably tight type annotations. If a library returns objects of a given type, it should expose those types so users can annotate code against them.

For example:
```python
    def softplus_distribution(x: ContinuousDistribution, /) -> ContinuousDistribution:
        ...
```
Without public distribution classes, users either have to omit useful annotations, use overly broad types, or import private implementation details.

### 3. Writing transforms

I also need a public way to construct monotonic transformations of distributions. The existing internal `MonotonicTransformedDistribution` expresses this directly, e.g.
```python
    def softplus_distribution(x: ContinuousDistribution, /) -> ContinuousDistribution:
        return MonotonicTransformedDistribution(
            x,
            g=softplus,
            h=inverse_softplus,
            dh=abs_derivative_inverse_softplus,
        )
```
but it is not public.

## Examples

Concrete examples from my codebase:

- https://github.com/NeilGirdhar/efax/blob/be0158e5bdcba8dd9fac8b4ee1a1a8f0c315e184/tests/softplus.py#L15
- https://github.com/NeilGirdhar/efax/blob/be0158e5bdcba8dd9fac8b4ee1a1a8f0c315e184/tests/match_scipy/test_pdf.py#L52

I am not asking that all private distribution infrastructure be made public. The specific request is to expose the public-facing abstractions needed for `isinstance` checks, type annotations, and monotonic transforms: `ContinuousDistribution`, `DiscreteDistribution`, and `MonotonicTransformedDistribution` or an equivalent public transform constructor.

title: BUG?: nyquist frequency zeroed out for even sized signals in hilbert2
body:
Since gh-23247 hilbert2 uses the following code:
```
    k0, k1 = (N[0] + 1) // 2, (N[1] + 1) // 2

    if k0 > 1:  # condition k0 > 1 needed for Dask backend
        Xf[..., 1:k0, :] *= 2.0
    if k1 > 1:  # condition k1 > 1 needed for Dask backend
        Xf[..., :, 1:k1] *= 2.0
    Xf[..., k0:, :] = 0.0
    Xf[..., :, k1:] = 0.0
```
which is in contrast to hilbert using:
```
    if N % 2 == 0:
        Xf[..., 1: N // 2] *= 2.0
        Xf[..., N // 2 + 1:N] = 0.0
    else:
        Xf[..., 1:(N + 1) // 2] *= 2.0
        Xf[..., (N + 1) // 2:N] = 0.0
```
Now, for even `N`, the code from hilbert would leave `N // 2` unchanged, while the code in hilbert2 uses `(N + 1) // 2 == N // 2 (even N)` sets it to 0.
I.e. for `hilbert2` for even sized lengths, the nyquist frequency component is zeroed rather than kept at a factor of 1.

I believe this is a regression in SciPy, I found this trying to check CuPy against SciPy 1.17 and where the test (added as a companion to the original SciPy PR no less) started failing.

I would think the correct thing is something like `(N + 1) // 1` for the `* 2` and `N // 2 + 1` for the zeroing, which then gives the identical value for odd `N` but leaves `N // 2` untouched for even.

CC @JesseLivezey

You can reproduce this example by temporarily adding @reproduce_failure('6.152.9', b'AXicc2G2PCjuyACCEAQjADY6A2E=') as a decorator on your test case
```

### SciPy/NumPy/Python version and system information

```shell
SciPy version: 1.18.0.dev0+git20260519.851124c
Numpy version: numpy-2.5.0.dev0
```

### Hypothesis version change

Looking at the differences in the two log files, the working one has hypothesis==6.152.8, and the failing one has hypothesis==6.152.9. Using the local `act` command above, I tested that this works locally in hypothesis 6.152.8 and fails in 6.152.9.

Here is the changelog for this version.

> Hypothesis 6.x
> 6.152.9 - 2026-05-19
> 
> This release substantially improves our internal distribution for generating integers. This release has the most visible effect on [integers()](https://hypothesis.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/strategies.html#hypothesis.strategies.integers), but may incidentally improve other strategies which draw integers internally.
> 
> Our integers distribution had two problems. First, it had jagged discontinuities at certain values where we switched sampling approaches. Second, it used a different distribution for bounded and unbounded ranges, which resulted in st.integers() and st.integers(-2**64, 2**64) producing very different distributions despite being semantically similar.
> 
> We now use a smooth distribution for both st.integers() and st.integers(a, b), which fixes both of these issues. This should substantially improve our testing power in certain cases.
> 
> The only way this release should be user-visible is that it finds more bugs! If this release is user-visible in other ways - for example, because it is slower, or produces a worse distribution in some cases - please open an issue.

title: API/DEP: special: rename `inv_boxcox{1p}` to `boxcox{1p}inv`
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

`special.inv_boxcox` and `special.inv_boxcox1p` are the only inverse functions in `scipy.special` that use an inv_ prefix. 

Every other inverse follows the *inv suffix convention: `erfinv`, `erfcinv`, `betaincinv`, etc

### Describe the solution you'd like.

Introduce `boxcoxinv` and `boxcox1pinv` as the canonical names, and deprecate `inv_boxcox` / `inv_boxcox1p` over the standard SciPy deprecation cycle. 

### Describe alternatives you've considered.

_No response_

### Additional context (e.g. screenshots, GIFs)

Noticed it while working on https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/25188

title: DOC: spatial.transform.RigidTransform.from_exp_coords: incorrect tau matrix definition
body:
### Issue with current documentation:

The documentation for scipy.spatial.transform.RigidTransform.from_exp_coords mistakenly defines the 4x4 matrix tau as 
```
tau = [  0 -rz  ry vx]
      [ rz   0 -rx vy]
      [-ry  rx   0 vz]
      [  0   0   0  1]
```



### Idea or request for content:

The tau matrix  should be defined as
```
tau = [  0 -rz  ry vx]
      [ rz   0 -rx vy]
      [-ry  rx   0 vz]
      [  0   0   0  0]
```
See [Lynch and Park (2017)](https://hades.mech.northwestern.edu/images/2/2e/MR-largefont-v2.pdf), Eq. 3.85.


### Additional context (e.g. screenshots, GIFs)

The following example shows that the mistake is limited to the documentation. The library itself is constructing the transform correctly. 

```
import numpy as np
from scipy.linalg import expm
from scipy.spatial.transform import RigidTransform

np.set_printoptions(suppress=True, precision=3)

rx, ry, rz = 1, 2, 3
vx, vy, vz = 4, 5, 6

transform = RigidTransform.from_exp_coords([rx, ry, rz, vx, vy, vz])
print(transform.as_matrix())  # Computed transform
# [[-0.695,  0.714,  0.089,  1.636],
#  [-0.192, -0.304,  0.933,  5.289],
#  [ 0.693,  0.631,  0.348,  6.595],
#  [ 0.   ,  0.   ,  0.   ,  1.   ]]

# Definition of tau per scipy documentation
tau = np.array([[  0, -rz,  ry, vx],
                [ rz,   0, -rx, vy],
                [-ry,  rx,   0, vz],
                [  0,   0,   0,  1]])
print(expm(tau))  # DOES NOT match computed transform
# [[-0.695,  0.714,  0.089,  3.352],
#  [-0.192, -0.304,  0.933,  9.374],
#  [ 0.693,  0.631,  0.348, 10.962],
#  [ 0.   , -0.   ,  0.   ,  2.718]]

# Correct formulation of tau
# Matches Lynch, K. M., & Park, F. C. (2017) Equation 3.85 
# https://hades.mech.northwestern.edu/images/2/2e/MR-largefont-v2.pdf
tau = np.array([[  0, -rz,  ry, vx],
                [ rz,   0, -rx, vy],
                [-ry,  rx,   0, vz],
                [  0,   0,   0,  0]])
print(expm(tau))  # DOES match computed transform
# [[-0.695,  0.714,  0.089,  1.636],
#  [-0.192, -0.304,  0.933,  5.289],
#  [ 0.693,  0.631,  0.348,  6.595],
#  [-0.   , -0.   , -0.   ,  1.   ]])
```

title: BUG: sparse: check for valid dtype in all constructor use patterns
body:
While reviewing the recent deprecation PR #25245, I found a number of cases where we don't check for a valid dtype when constructing sparse arrays. For example,
```python
sp.sparse.csr_array((np.array([None]), np.array([0]), np.array([0, 1]))) # allows dtype='object' 
sp.sparse.csr_array((np.array([1], dtype=np.float16), np.array([0]), np.array([0, 1]))) # allows dtype='float16'
```
 
These constructions are allowed and seem to work, but then we raise a TypeError when any operation on the sparse array checks the dtype. For example, `str(A)` raises a TypeError indicating an unsupported dtype.

I propose that we make a follow-up to #20754 which added checks to each `__init__` call, but seems to have missed cases when `dtype` is not specified.  That is, when `dtype=` is specified we check whether that is supported. But when `dtype=None` and a dtype is inferred from input arrays we don't check that the resulting dtype is supported.  

I think the `_sputils.getdtype` helper function should be sufficient. But we will need to find all the ways it might arise -- probably with a test over all formats using `dtype=None` and input data of 'float16' and/or 'object'.

title: BUG: interpolate._fitpack2: reference counting audit
body:
### Describe your issue.

While following up on a free-threading crash, I was attempting to run a set of tests over and over in a loop.

```
spin test -t 'scipy/stats/tests/test_distributions.py::TestLevyStable::test_cdf_nolan_samples' -m full -- --forever
```

(Requires pytest-run-parallel to be installed, though this is not using threads.)

Curiously, this runs out of memory and crashes after 98 iterations on a computer with 12GB of RAM.

[Analyzing this under memray](https://gist.github.com/nickodell/372c483205a64b2d16fd550098da7cdd), the largest contributor to memory use is this line:

https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/84008d298c6be5843bfe54c6b2cad6587159f25e/scipy/interpolate/_fitpack2.py#L118

With 7.5 GB used after 50 iterations.

I modified _fitpack2.py to print the reference count of wrk before and after entering `_fitpack.curfit`. Calling this function causes it to gain one reference. Similarly, iwrk gains one reference too.

The following modification to `_curfit()` causes the memory usage of that stats test to become stable. It also passes all tests.

```
diff --git a/scipy/interpolate/src/_fitpackmodule.c b/scipy/interpolate/src/_fitpackmodule.c
index b82640bd5d..00de650ddf 100644
--- a/scipy/interpolate/src/_fitpackmodule.c
+++ b/scipy/interpolate/src/_fitpackmodule.c
@@ -973,6 +973,8 @@ fitpack_curfit(PyObject* Py_UNUSED(dummy), PyObject *args)
     Py_DECREF(ap_y);
     Py_DECREF(ap_w);
     /* Don't decref ap_t, ap_wrk, ap_iwrk - they are modified in-place */
+    Py_DECREF(ap_wrk);
+    Py_DECREF(ap_iwrk);
 
     return Py_BuildValue(("iNNdi"),
                          n, PyArray_Return(ap_t), PyArray_Return(ap_c), fp, ier);
```

... but you'll notice a comment there that the author explicitly considered decref'ing ap_wrk and that doing so is incorrect. 

(As far as I can tell, the comment is correct with respect to `ap_t`. It steals a reference to return to the caller as `t_out`, so it would not be correct to decref `ap_t` here, and doing so immediately crashes.)



### Reproducing Code Example

The following test command runs out of memory and crashes.

```bash
spin test -t 'scipy/stats/tests/test_distributions.py::TestLevyStable::test_cdf_nolan_samples' -m full -- --forever
```

### Error message

```shell
N/A
```

title: BUG: Inconsistent behavior between `stats.tvar` and `mstats.tvar`
body:
### Describe your issue.

`scipy.stats.mstats.tvar` returns `inf` (with a divide-by-zero warning) when applied to a masked array with no masked elements. The documentation suggests that `mstats.tvar` is expected to behave consistently with `scipy.stats.tvar` in cases where no masked elements are present.

However, the same input produces a finite and consistent result with `scipy.stats.tvar` and `numpy.var`

Similar to: #24847 

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats

arr = np.ma.array([1, 0, 1, 1, 0], dtype=np.int32)

print("mstats.tvar:", stats.mstats.tvar(arr))
print("stats.tvar  :", stats.tvar(arr))
print("np.var      :", np.var(arr.data, ddof=1))
```

### Error message

```shell
mstats.tvar: inf
stats.tvar  : 0.30000000000000004
np.var      : 0.30000000000000004
... site-packages/scipy/stats/_mstats_basic.py:2579: RuntimeWarning: divide by zero encountered in scalar divide
  return np.ma.var(a) * n/(n-1.)
```

### SciPy/NumPy/Python version and system information

```shell
1.19.0.dev0+git20260601.ff7b69a 2.4.6 sys.version_info(major=3, minor=12, micro=3, releaselevel='final', serial=0)
```

### Tool-Use Disclosure
This issue was identified by an automated testing tool currently being developed by our research team. 
Before reporting it, we reviewed the documentation, code and checked a recent development version of SciPy to confirm the issue.

title: BUG: `ndimage.maximum_position` result depends on values outside the requested label
body:
### Describe your issue.

`scipy.ndimage.maximum_position(input, labels, index)` should return "the positions of the maximums of the values of `input` inside the regions defined by `labels`. Only pixels where `labels == index` should contribute. In practice, modifying `input` at pixels where `labels != index` can change the returned position even though every value inside the requested region is unchanged.

-------
Surfaced in a downstream project [centrosome](https://github.com/CellProfiler/centrosome/issues/130), [cp_measure](https://github.com/afermg/cp_measure/issues/22).

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
from scipy.ndimage import maximum_position

img = np.zeros((10, 10))
img[2:5, 2:5] = 1.0   # plateau under label 1
img[7:9, 7:9] = 5.0   # values under label 2 — should be irrelevant for index=[1]

labels = np.zeros((10, 10), dtype=int)
labels[2:5, 2:5] = 1
labels[7:9, 7:9] = 2

pos_with    = maximum_position(img, labels, [1])

img_zeroed = img.copy()
img_zeroed[labels == 2] = 0   # change only pixels outside label 1
pos_without = maximum_position(img_zeroed, labels, [1])

print("with label-2 values  :", pos_with)     # -> [(3, 3)]
print("without label-2 values:", pos_without) # -> [(2, 4)]
assert pos_with == pos_without, (pos_with, pos_without)
```

### Error message

```shell
with label-2 values  : [(3, 3)]
without label-2 values: [(2, 4)]
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "repro.py", line 19, in <module>
    assert pos_with == pos_without, (pos_with, pos_without)
AssertionError: ([(3, 3)], [(2, 4)])
```

title: BUG: special: `spherical_in` returns 0 instead of the correct small value
body:
An XSLOW test that has been failing for a very long time in `scipy.special` is `TestSystematic::spherical_in` (see https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/15384). A test case that fails is `spherical_in(10, 5.2250558491838786e-27)`. It returns `0`, but the correct result (computed with `mpmath`) is `1.1031344476093099e-273`. It turns out there is a small value of `x` below which `spherical_in(10, x)` returns `0` instead of the correct value. The script below generates the following plot: ### Reproducing Code Example ```python import numpy as np import scipy from scipy.special import spherical_in import mpmath from mpmath import mp import matplotlib.pyplot as plt # From test_mpmath.py: @mp.workdps(50) def mp_spherical_in(n, z): arg = mpmath.mpmathify(z) out = (mpmath.besseli(n + mpmath.mpf(1)/2, arg) / mpmath.sqrt(2*arg/mpmath.pi)) if arg.imag == 0: return out.real else: return out x = np.linspace(1.0e-26, 1.35e-26, 2000) y = spherical_in(10, x) ref = np.array([float(mp_spherical_in(10, t)) for t in x]) plt.plot(x, y, linewidth=3, label='SciPy') plt.plot(x, ref, 'k:', alpha=0.75, label='mpmath') plt.title(f'special.spherical_in(10, x)\n(SciPy {scipy.__version__})') plt.xlabel('x') plt.legend(framealpha=1, shadow=True) plt.grid(alpha=0.5) plt.show() ```
### SciPy/NumPy/Python version and system information ```shell 1.19.0.dev0+git20260602.f8dd492 2.4.6 sys.version_info(major=3, minor=14, micro=5, releaselevel='final', serial=0) Build Dependencies: blas: cython blas ilp64: false detection method: pkgconfig found: true has ilp64: false include directory: /usr/include/x86_64-linux-gnu/openblas-pthread/ lib directory: /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/openblas-pthread/ name: openblas openblas configuration: USE_64BITINT=0 DYNAMIC_ARCH=1 DYNAMIC_OLDER=1 NO_CBLAS= NO_LAPACK= NO_LAPACKE=1 NO_AFFINITY=1 USE_OPENMP=0 generic MAX_THREADS=64 pc file directory: /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/pkgconfig version: 0.3.26 lapack: detection method: pkgconfig found: true has ilp64: false include directory: /usr/include/x86_64-linux-gnu/openblas-pthread/ lib directory: /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/openblas-pthread/ name: openblas openblas configuration: USE_64BITINT=0 DYNAMIC_ARCH=1 DYNAMIC_OLDER=1 NO_CBLAS= NO_LAPACK= NO_LAPACKE=1 NO_AFFINITY=1 USE_OPENMP=0 generic MAX_THREADS=64 pc file directory: /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/pkgconfig version: 0.3.26 pybind11: detection method: config-tool include directory: unknown name: pybind11 version: 3.0.4 Compilers: c: commands: cc linker: ld.bfd name: gcc version: 13.3.0 c++: commands: c++ linker: ld.bfd name: gcc version: 13.3.0 cython: commands: cython linker: cython name: cython version: 3.2.4 pythran: include directory: ../../../../../py3.14.5/lib/python3.14/site-packages/pythran version: 0.18.1 Machine Information: build: cpu: x86_64 endian: little family: x86_64 system: linux cross-compiled: false host: cpu: x86_64 endian: little family: x86_64 system: linux Python Information: path: /home/warren/py3.14.5/bin/python3.14 version: '3.14' ```

title: ENH: Add lyubishchev_divergence to scipy.spatial.distance — continuous-measurement analogue of sokalsneath/sokalmichener
body:
**Related:** `scipy.spatial.distance.sokalsneath`, `scipy.spatial.distance.sokalmichener`

---

#### Summary

`scipy.spatial.distance` includes `sokalsneath` and `sokalmichener` — dissimilarity functions for binary presence/absence vectors, named after Sokal & Sneath (1963). There is no continuous-measurement analogue with covariance structure in scipy

Lyubishchev (1943) defined exactly this — a divergence coefficient for continuous measurements, extended to multivariate data with the full Edgeworth-Pearson covariance formulation. He published in *Biometrics* (1962), one year before Sokal & Sneath's *Principles of Numerical Taxonomy* (1963). His name appears nowhere in scipy despite predating the functions that do appear there

This issue proposes adding `lyubishchev_divergence` to `scipy.spatial.distance`

---

#### Proposed function

```python
scipy.spatial.distance.lyubishchev_divergence(a, b)
```

Lyubishchev's D from his 1943 manuscript:

```
D = (M₁ - M₂)² / (σ₁² + σ₂²)
```

Squared distance between two group means, normalized by pooled variance. Works on continuous measurements. When D > 1.0 groups are cleanly separated. When D < 0.5 there is transgression — the boundary between groups breaks down

**Why this is not redundant with existing functions:**

| | `sokalsneath` / `sokalmichener` | `lyubishchev_divergence` |
|---|---|---|
| Input | Binary presence/absence vectors | Continuous measurements |
| Covariance | Not modeled (independence assumed) | Full covariance matrix |
| Distance metric | Simple matching / weighted mismatch | Mahalanobis distance |

Lyubishchev explicitly criticized the independence assumption in his 1943 manuscript. The covariance structure between measurements is not noise — it is information

---

#### Working implementation

A working implementation with tests is available at:

https://github.com/AkzhanBerdi/lyubishchev

The relevant function is `divergence_coefficient` in `lyubishchev/core.py`. It handles univariate and multivariate input, edge cases (constant features, single samples), and is tested against known values

---

#### Primary sources

> Lyubishchev, A.A. (1943). *Programma obshchey sistematiki* [Program of General Systematics]
> Manuscript, 22 November 1943. Digitized by ZIN RAS Coleoptera Laboratory
> http://www.zin.ru/animalia/coleoptera/rus/lyubis05.htm

> Lubischew, A.A. (1962). On the use of discriminant functions in taxonomy
> *Biometrics*, 18(4), 455–477

---

#### Historical note

`sokalsneath` and `sokalmichener` are named after researchers who published in 1963. Lyubishchev published the continuous-measurement generalization of the same problem in 1962, with the theoretical framework in a 1943 manuscript. He has no presence in scipy. This proposal corrects that

title: BUG: wrong dtype when computing the kronecker product/sum if there is a zero matrix
body:
### Describe your issue.

Tested on scipy 1.17.1.
```python
import numpy as np
import scipy.sparse as sp

A = sp.csr_matrix(np.array([[0]], dtype=np.int64))
B = sp.csr_matrix(np.array([[3]], dtype=np.int64))

kr = sp.kron(A, B)
print(A.dtype, B.dtype)
print(kr.dtype)
```
And it outputs
```
int64 int64
float64
```
The result `kr` has dtype `float64`, but I think the correct output dtype should be `int64`. It seems a bug when there is a zero matrix in the `kron` input.

The function `kronsum` has the same problem also.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
import scipy.sparse as sp

A = sp.csr_matrix(np.array([[0]], dtype=np.int64))
B = sp.csr_matrix(np.array([[3]], dtype=np.int64))

kr = sp.kron(A, B)
print(A.dtype, B.dtype)
print(kr.dtype)
```

```shell
int64 int64
float64
```

title: BUG: Incorrect slope edge case (non-zero value for identical y values)
body:
### Describe your issue.

Identified an edge case where the slope is being reported as non-zero (though very small) for identical values of y. I don't know whether or not the algorithm is guaranteed to return a zero slope in this situation. For higher values of y the problem is resolved (but pvalue returns nan - expected?). Putting this in in case it reveals a latent bug.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
from scipy.stats import linregress
x = list(range(1778328000,1778875200,3600)) + list(range(1779087600,1780920000,3600))
y = [66094598367756] * 661
print(linregress(x,y).slope, linregress(x,y).pvalue)
y = [p + 20000000000000 for p in y]
print(linregress(x,y).slope, linregress(x,y).pvalue)
# 8.364264261336637e-22 0.999999999998346
# 0.0 nan

title: MAINT, TYP: some typing-related concerns in 1.18.x
body:
In gh-25297, I decided that it would be best to move forward with `1.18.0` RC2 sooner than later, but there are two typing-related issues that we are thankful that Joren highlighted for us and that we may want to consider fixing up one way or another, even if that has to wait until i.e., `1.18.1`. I'm just going to link to some relevant content for the two items below, otherwise they'll fall off the radar.

1. For 2D input, the return type of `scipy.linalg.bandwidth` has changed from ``(int, int)`` to ``(np.int64, np.int64)``.  Related discussion at https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/25297#discussion_r3351844204 and original identification of issue at: https://github.com/scipy/scipy-stubs/pull/1624.

2. The types of the ``k`` and ``n`` attributes of the ``BinomTestResult`` object returned by `scipy.stats.binomtest` have changed from ``int`` to ``np.float64``. Not much discussion from domain experts on this one yet. Original stubs PR identifying the problem is here: https://github.com/scipy/scipy-stubs/pull/1683.

title: BUG: special.chdtr{c}: `dask` output shape mismatch
body:
NumPy:

```
(Pdb) self.df.shape
(2, 1)
(Pdb) x.shape
(2,)
(Pdb) special.chdtrc(self.df, x).shape
(2, 2)
```

Dask:

```
(Pdb) self.df
dask.array<sub, shape=(2, 1), dtype=float64, chunksize=(2, 1), chunktype=numpy.ndarray>
(Pdb) !x
dask.array<sum-aggregate, shape=(2,), dtype=float64, chunksize=(2,), chunktype=numpy.ndarray>
(Pdb) special.chdtrc(self.df, x).shape
(2, 1)
```

discovered while reviewing gh-25143, this causes

```
scipy/special/tests/test_support_alternative_backends.py::test_chdtr_gh21311[dask.array]
scipy/stats/tests/test_stats.py::TestPowerDivergence::test_ddof_broadcasting[dask.array]
```

to fail.

@fbourgey might you be interested in taking a look?

title: BUG: ndimage measurement functions reject a list index under the array API (Multiple namespaces)
body:
### Describe your issue.

With `SCIPY_ARRAY_API=1` and a non-NumPy array API `input`/`labels` (jax, torch, cupy, array_api_strict, dask) passing a plain python list as `index` to the labeled measurement functions raises `TypeError: Multiple namespaces for array inputs` even though `index` is only a label selector and is documented as an int or sequence of ints.

The cause is in `scipy/ndimage/_delegators.py`. `maximum_signature` resolves the array namespace from `index` along with `input` and `labels`:

```python
def maximum_signature(input, labels=None, index=None):
    return array_namespace(input, labels, index)
```

A plain list is treated as NumPy so with a non-NumPy `input` the namespace set has two entries and `array_namespace` raises. A scalar `index` is fine (scalars are ignored) and an `index` array in the same namespace as `input` is fine.

`maximum_signature` is aliased to the whole measurement family so this affects `minimum`, `maximum`, `median`, `mean`, `variance`, `standard_deviation`, `sum`/`sum_labels`, `minimum_position`, `maximum_position`, `extrema`, and `center_of_mass`.

`histogram_signature` already omits `index` and is not affected:

```python
def histogram_signature(input, min, max, bins, labels=None, index=None):
    return array_namespace(input, labels)
```

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import os
os.environ["SCIPY_ARRAY_API"] = "1"

import array_api_strict as xp
import scipy.ndimage as ndi

x = xp.asarray([[1., 1., 0.], [0., 0., 2.]])
labels = xp.asarray([[1, 1, 0], [0, 0, 2]])

# Works: index in the same namespace as the input
ndi.maximum_position(x, labels, xp.asarray([1, 2]))

# Raises: a plain Python list is treated as a second (NumPy) namespace
ndi.maximum_position(x, labels, [1, 2])
```

### Error message

```shell
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/ledarrow/.cache/uv/archive-v0/4MZoWI5P4sZOi7GC/lib/python3.11/site-packages/scipy/_lib/array_api_compat/common/_helpers.py", line 669, in array_namespace
    (xp,) = namespaces
    ^^^^^
ValueError: too many values to unpack (expected 1)

During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 8, in <module>
  File "/home/ledarrow/.cache/uv/archive-v0/4MZoWI5P4sZOi7GC/lib/python3.11/site-packages/scipy/ndimage/_support_alternative_backends.py", line 41, in wrapper
    xp = delegator(*args, **kwds)
         ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/home/ledarrow/.cache/uv/archive-v0/4MZoWI5P4sZOi7GC/lib/python3.11/site-packages/scipy/ndimage/_delegators.py", line 205, in maximum_signature
    return array_namespace(input, labels, index)
           ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/home/ledarrow/.cache/uv/archive-v0/4MZoWI5P4sZOi7GC/lib/python3.11/site-packages/scipy/_lib/_array_api_override.py", line 150, in array_namespace
    return array_api_compat.array_namespace(*numpy_arrays, *api_arrays)
           ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/home/ledarrow/.cache/uv/archive-v0/4MZoWI5P4sZOi7GC/lib/python3.11/site-packages/scipy/_lib/array_api_compat/common/_helpers.py", line 676, in array_namespace
    raise TypeError(f"Multiple namespaces for array inputs: {namespaces}")
TypeError: Multiple namespaces for array inputs: {<module 'array_api_strict' from '/home/ledarrow/.cache/uv/archive-v0/4MZoWI5P4sZOi7GC/lib/python3.11/site-packages/array_api_strict/__init__.py'>, <module 'scipy._lib.array_api_compat.numpy' from '/home/ledarrow/.cache/uv/archive-v0/4MZoWI5P4sZOi7GC/lib/python3.11/site-packages/scipy/_lib/array_api_compat/numpy/__init__.py'>}
```

### SciPy/NumPy/Python version and system information

```shell
1.17.1 2.4.6 sys.version_info(major=3, minor=11, micro=15, releaselevel='final', serial=0)
array_api_strict 2.5

```

Also reproduces on `main` seen across jax, torch, cupy, array_api_strict, and dask in CI (PR #25293). 

The fix looks small just drop `index` from `maximum_signature` so it matches `histogram_signature` since the result namespace comes from `input`/`labels` and `index` is only a selector. 

I can open a PR for that or I could coerce `index` into the input namespace instead, happy to go either way.

title: BUG: `scipy/integrate/__quadpack.c` — `dqk15w` forward declaration uses K&R `double()`, fails to compile under C23
body:
### Describe your issue.

On commit `d4a7a2463e1eb2825848a61f6913013fd1f5c21b` `scipy/integrate/__quadpack.c` does not compile with C23. The forward declaration of the internal `dqk15w` routine declares its weight-function parameter with the empty-parentheses K&R form `double()`. 

In c23 an empty parameter list means `(void)` (not "unspecified arguments" as in C17 and earlier). So the forward declaration's second parameter becomes `double (*)(void)`, which conflicts with
the function's own definition (which uses `quadpack_w_func`) and rejects the weight functions passed at every
call site.

The file already defines the proper weight-function type and uses it in `dqk15w`'s definition. Only the
forward declaration seems to be out of sync.

Under C17 the K&R `double()` was compatible with `quadpack_w_func` ("unspecified args"), so this compiled. Under C23, `double()` is `double(void)`, so the declaration and definition disagree.


The fix is to ,ake the forward declaration use the same `quadpack_w_func` type as the definition. This is C17 and 
C23-correct.

Current sed patch: 
```sh
sed -i 's/,double(),/,quadpack_w_func,/' scipy/integrate/__quadpack.c
```

### Reproducing Code Example

```bash
# Compile from sources with C23.

pip3 install . --compile --no-dependencies \ 
  -Csetup-args="-Dblas=openblas" \
  -Csetup-args="-Dlapack=openblas" \
  -Csetup-args="-Duse-pythran=true" \
  -Csetup-args=-Db_colorout=always \
  -Csetup-args=-Dc_std=gnu23 \
  -Csetup-args=-Dcpp_std=gnu++17 # Must downgrade to c++17 which is a separate issue
```

### Error message

```shell
scipy/integrate/__quadpack.c:3255:21: error: incompatible function pointer types passing
      'double (const double, const double, const double, const double, const double, const int)'
      to parameter of type 'double (*)(void)' [-Wincompatible-function-pointer-types]
scipy/integrate/__quadpack.c:3447:21: error: incompatible function pointer types ...
scipy/integrate/__quadpack.c:4102:17: error: incompatible function pointer types ...
scipy/integrate/__quadpack.c:4874:1: error: conflicting types for 'dqk15w'
```

### SciPy/NumPy/Python version and system information

```shell
Commit `d4a7a2463e1eb2825848a61f6913013fd1f5c21b`
Clang20
```

title: TASK: special: Review error and exception handling of Boost wrappers
body:
Over in https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/25384, @j-bowhay [commented](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/25384#discussion_r3418978721) on the error handling in a wrapper of a Boost function:

> @steppi I wanted to ask you about this pattern as it is wide spread in the boost wrappers. In the wrappers if we receive an underflow/overflow error the wrappers returns 0/something small/inf but to me it is not obvious this is correct. What if it an intermediate calculation that under/overflows that doesn't mean the final answer is 0/inf?
> 
> P.S. sorry @dschmitz89 I know we have discussed this once before but it still bugs me

The problem is to ensure that the mapping from a Boost exception to the return value and error state of the wrapper in `scipy.special` is correct.

title: [BUG] SuperLUGlobal_dealloc reads value instead of key - all SuperLU memory leaked
body:
## Bug Description

In `scipy/sparse/linalg/_dsolve/_superlu_utils.c`, the `SuperLUGlobal_dealloc` function reads the dict **value** instead of the **key** when trying to free allocated memory. Since values are `Py_None`, all SuperLU memory is leaked.

## Location

`scipy/sparse/linalg/_dsolve/_superlu_utils.c:165-169`

## Code

```c
while (PyDict_Next(self->memory_dict, &pos, &key, &value)) {
    void *ptr;
    ptr = PyLong_AsVoidPtr(value);  // BUG: should be 'key'!
    free(ptr);
}
```

## Context

In `superlu_python_module_malloc` (line 108-111), entries are stored as:
```c
PyDict_SetItem(g->memory_dict, PyLong_FromVoidPtr(mem_ptr), Py_None);
```

The allocated pointer is stored in the **key** (as a PyLong), while the **value** is always `Py_None`. The dealloc reads `value` → `PyLong_AsVoidPtr(Py_None)` → TypeError (returns NULL with exception set) → `free(NULL)` (no-op).

## Impact

**ALL memory allocated by SuperLU is leaked** when the `SuperLUGlobal` object is deallocated. This affects every sparse linear solve operation using SuperLU (e.g., `scipy.sparse.linalg.spsolve`). Memory grows unboundedly with repeated solves.

## Suggested Fix

```c
ptr = PyLong_AsVoidPtr(key);  // key holds the pointer, not value
```

## Environment

- SciPy version: current main
- Affected: `scipy.sparse.linalg.spsolve()` and all SuperLU-based solvers

title: [BUG] Py_XDECREF on borrowed refs in fitpack_sphere (iopt==-1) - use-after-free
body:
## Bug Description

In `scipy/interpolate/src/_fitpackmodule.c`, when `iopt == -1`, `ap_tt` and `ap_tp` are assigned as borrowed references (no INCREF). If an error later triggers `goto fail`, the fail path does `Py_XDECREF` on these borrowed refs, corrupting their refcount.

## Location

`scipy/interpolate/src/_fitpackmodule.c:1779-1784, 1854-1860`

## Code

```c
// When iopt == -1:
ap_tt = tt;   // borrowed reference, no INCREF
ap_tp = tp;   // borrowed reference, no INCREF

// ... if error occurs ...
goto fail;

fail:
    Py_XDECREF(ap_tt);  // BUG: decrementing borrowed reference!
    Py_XDECREF(ap_tp);  // BUG: decrementing borrowed reference!
```

## Impact

Decrementing a borrowed reference can drop the refcount to 0, freeing the object while the caller still holds a reference. This causes use-after-free or crash on the next access. Triggers on any validation error in `_spherefit_lsq` with `iopt=-1`.

## Suggested Fix

Add `Py_INCREF` when borrowing:
```c
ap_tt = tt;  Py_INCREF(ap_tt);
ap_tp = tp;  Py_INCREF(ap_tp);
```

## Environment

- SciPy version: current main
- Affected: `scipy.interpolate.SmoothSphereBivariateSpline` with `iopt=-1`

title: [BUG] Double-free of wrk1/wrk2/iwrk in fitpack_surfit on allocation failure
body:
## Bug Description

In `scipy/interpolate/src/_fitpackmodule.c`, after the Fortran `surfit()` call, `wrk1`/`wrk2`/`iwrk` are freed but NOT set to NULL. If a subsequent `PyArray_SimpleNew` fails, `goto fail` frees them again.

## Location

`scipy/interpolate/src/_fitpackmodule.c:1578-1580, 1610-1618`

## Code

```c
free(wrk1);    // line 1578: freed
free(wrk2);    // line 1579: freed  
free(iwrk);    // line 1580: freed
// NOT set to NULL!

// ... if PyArray_SimpleNew fails later:
goto fail;

fail:
    free(wrk1);   // line 1610: DOUBLE FREE
    free(wrk2);   // line 1615: DOUBLE FREE
    free(iwrk);   // line 1618: DOUBLE FREE
```

## Impact

Double-free causes heap corruption. On modern allocators (glibc, jemalloc), this typically causes:
- Immediate crash (`double free detected`)
- Silent heap corruption leading to later crashes
- Potential security vulnerability (CWE-415)

Triggers when `PyArray_SimpleNew` fails due to OOM after the Fortran call.

## Suggested Fix

```c
free(wrk1); wrk1 = NULL;
free(wrk2); wrk2 = NULL;
free(iwrk); iwrk = NULL;
```

## Environment

- SciPy version: current main
- Affected: `scipy.interpolate.bisplrep` / surface fitting with OOM
